title: EASA Foundation
description: Master the essential aviation knowledge required for all EASA drone certifications. Covers airspace, meteorology, aerodynamics, drone systems, regulations, and safety — aligned with EU Regulations 2019/947 and 2019/945.

modules.title: Aviation Fundamentals
modules.description: Core aviation knowledge for drone pilots: airspace classification, weather interpretation, multirotor aerodynamics, and flight planning with NOTAMs.

lessons.title: Airspace Classification
lessons.detailed_explanation:
Understanding airspace classification is one of the most critical competencies for any drone pilot operating in Europe. The International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) defines seven classes of airspace — designated A through G — each with distinct rules governing who may enter, what equipment is required, and how air traffic control (ATC) services are provided. For drone operators, this classification system determines where you can fly, what authorisations you need, and how you interact with manned aviation. In the European Union, EASA's regulatory framework (specifically EU 2019/947 and EU 2019/945) builds upon this ICAO foundation, adding drone-specific rules such as the 120-metre above ground level (AGL) height limit for Open Category operations and the concept of UAS geographical zones.

Airspace is broadly divided into two categories: controlled and uncontrolled. Controlled airspace — which includes Control Zones (CTR) around airports, Terminal Manoeuvring Areas (TMA) at higher altitudes above airports, and Aerodrome Traffic Zones (ATZ) — is managed by air traffic control services that separate aircraft and manage traffic flow. Classes A through E fall within controlled airspace, with Class A being the most restrictive (no VFR flights permitted, IFR only with ATC clearance) and Class E being the least restrictive controlled airspace. For drone pilots, flying in controlled airspace almost always requires prior authorisation from the competent authority or ATC, even within the Open Category. Uncontrolled airspace, comprising Classes F and G, does not provide mandatory separation services but still has rules and traffic. Most recreational and commercial drone flights in the Open Category take place in Class G airspace below 120 metres AGL.

Under EU 2019/947 Article 15, EU Member States are empowered to designate UAS geographical zones where drone operations are restricted, limited, or facilitated. These zones can prohibit flights near sensitive areas such as government buildings, prisons, critical infrastructure, nature reserves, or densely populated areas. Conversely, they may also define zones where certain restrictions are relaxed. Drone pilots must consult their national aviation authority's geographical zone database before every flight. Class-marked drones (C1, C2, and C3 under EU 2019/945) are required to have geo-awareness systems that can load and display updated UAS geographical zone information, alerting pilots when they approach or enter restricted areas. C1, C2, and C3 drones must also be equipped with geofencing capability where mandated by the geographical zone.

Looking ahead, the European U-space framework represents a paradigm shift in how low-altitude airspace will be managed. U-space is a set of digital services and automated systems designed to ensure safe and efficient UAS operations, particularly in dense traffic environments. It will provide services such as electronic identification, geo-awareness, flight authorisation, and dynamic airspace management. As U-space matures through its four deployment phases (U1 through U4), drone pilots will interact with U-space Service Providers (USSPs) to file flight plans, receive real-time traffic information, and obtain automated clearances. Understanding these evolving airspace concepts is essential for any pilot preparing for the EASA A1/A3 competency certificate, as they form the backbone of safe and legal drone operations across Europe.

step_by_step_guide.title: Identify the ICAO Airspace Classes and Their Hierarchy
step_by_step_guide.description: Begin by learning the seven ICAO airspace classes (A through G) and their key characteristics. Study how each class differs in terms of ATC services provided, flight rules permitted (IFR/VFR), speed limits, radio communication requirements, and separation services. Create a reference card summarising each class.
step_by_step_guide.code:
ICAO Airspace Classes Summary:

Class A — IFR only, ATC clearance required, full separation provided
Class B — IFR and VFR, ATC clearance required, full separation provided
Class C — IFR and VFR, ATC clearance required, IFR/IFR and IFR/VFR separation
Class D — IFR and VFR, ATC clearance required, IFR/IFR separation, traffic info for VFR
Class E — IFR and VFR, ATC clearance for IFR only, IFR/IFR separation
Class F — IFR and VFR, no ATC clearance, advisory service where available
Class G — IFR and VFR, no ATC clearance, flight information service only

step_by_step_guide.title: Understand Controlled vs Uncontrolled Airspace Boundaries
step_by_step_guide.description: Study the different types of controlled airspace structures: Control Zones (CTR) extend from the surface around airports and typically have a radius of 5-10 NM. Terminal Manoeuvring Areas (TMA) sit above CTRs and manage arriving and departing traffic. Aerodrome Traffic Zones (ATZ) protect the immediate vicinity of aerodromes. Learn to identify their vertical and lateral boundaries on charts. Uncontrolled airspace (Class F and G) exists outside these structures.
step_by_step_guide.code:
Controlled Airspace Structures:

CTR (Control Zone):
  - Extends from ground level (SFC) upward
  - Typically 5-10 NM radius around an airport
  - Usually Class C or D
  - Drone flights require ATC authorisation

TMA (Terminal Manoeuvring Area):
  - Sits above the CTR
  - Manages approach/departure traffic
  - Usually Class A, B, or C

ATZ (Aerodrome Traffic Zone):
  - Protects aerodrome traffic patterns
  - Typically 2-2.5 NM radius, SFC to 2000ft AGL

FIR (Flight Information Region):
  - Encompasses all airspace in a country/region
  - Divided into upper and lower sections

step_by_step_guide.title: Locate UAS Geographical Zones Using Your National Authority's Map
step_by_step_guide.description: Visit your national aviation authority's official UAS geographical zone map or approved app. In many EU states these are available through dedicated drone apps (e.g., droneguide.eu, AIP Drone in France, Droneradar in Poland, Map2Fly in Germany). Identify no-fly zones, restricted zones, and zones with specific conditions. Practice looking up zones around your local area, noting the restrictions and any required authorisations.
step_by_step_guide.code:
Common UAS Geographical Zone Types:

- No-Fly Zones: Absolute prohibition (e.g., near airports, military areas)
- Restricted Zones: Flights allowed with specific authorisation only
- Height-Limited Zones: Maximum altitude lower than 120m AGL
- Time-Limited Zones: Restrictions during specific hours (e.g., NOTAMs)
- Facilitated Zones: Zones where certain restrictions are relaxed

Check these sources before EVERY flight:
1. National aviation authority UAS zone map
2. Active NOTAMs (Notices to Air Missions)
3. Local bylaws and municipality restrictions
4. Temporary flight restrictions (TFRs)

step_by_step_guide.title: Determine Legal Flight Areas Within the Open Category
step_by_step_guide.description: Apply the rules from EU 2019/947 to determine where you can legally fly your drone in the Open Category. The key rule is 120 metres AGL maximum height. However, you must also consider horizontal distance from people, assemblies, urban areas, and aerodromes. Cross-reference the subcategory of your operation (A1, A2, or A3) with the class marking of your drone (C0-C4) to understand exact limitations. Map out at least three locations near you where Open Category flights are permitted.
step_by_step_guide.code:
Open Category Rules Summary (EU 2019/947):

Subcategory A1 (over people, not assemblies):
  - C0 drones: <250g, no speed limit concern
  - C1 drones: <900g, max 19 m/s

Subcategory A2 (close to people, 30m/5m horizontal):
  - C2 drones: <4kg, low-speed mode available
  - Requires A2 competency certificate

Subcategory A3 (far from people):
  - C2, C3 (<25kg), C4 (<25kg) drones
  - 150m horizontal from residential, commercial, industrial, recreational areas

ALL subcategories:
  - Max height: 120m AGL
  - Visual line of sight (VLOS) required
  - Must not fly in controlled airspace without authorisation

step_by_step_guide.title: Read and Interpret a VFR Chart for Drone Operations
step_by_step_guide.description: Obtain a VFR (Visual Flight Rules) aeronautical chart for your region — many are available free through national aviation authorities or services like OpenAIP. Learn to identify controlled airspace boundaries (shown as blue or magenta lines with altitudes), aerodrome locations, restricted and danger areas, and airspace floor/ceiling altitudes. Practice identifying the airspace class at a specific location and altitude. Note that as a drone pilot, you typically operate well below the altitudes shown on VFR charts, but understanding them is essential for situational awareness.
step_by_step_guide.code:
VFR Chart Reading for Drone Pilots — Key Symbols:

- Blue dashed lines: CTR boundary (controlled airspace from surface)
- Blue solid lines: TMA boundary with floor/ceiling altitudes
- Magenta lines: Restricted or danger areas
- Circle with tower symbol: Aerodrome location
- Altitude labels: e.g., 'FL065 / 1500ft AMSL' (ceiling/floor)
- 'SFC' = Surface (airspace starts from ground level)
- 'GND' = Ground (same as surface)
- 'UNL' = Unlimited (airspace extends upward without limit)

For drone ops, focus on:
1. Is there controlled airspace above your location?
2. What is the floor of any controlled airspace?
3. Are there restricted/danger areas nearby?

step_by_step_guide.title: Explore Geofencing and Geo-Awareness Requirements
step_by_step_guide.description: Study the geofencing and geo-awareness requirements for class-marked drones under EU 2019/945. Geo-awareness means the drone can upload and display UAS geographical zone information, warning the pilot about nearby restrictions. Geofencing goes further — it physically prevents the drone from entering a restricted zone. Practice checking your drone's geofencing settings, updating its database, and understanding what happens when your drone approaches a geofenced boundary.
step_by_step_guide.code:
Geo-Awareness vs Geofencing:

Geo-Awareness (required for C1, C2, C3):
  - Drone loads UAS geographical zone data
  - Displays warnings to the pilot
  - Does NOT physically prevent entry
  - Pilot retains control and responsibility
  - Database must be updatable by the pilot

Geofencing (required when mandated by geographical zone):
  - Drone physically prevents entry into the restricted zone
  - Automated boundary enforcement
  - Required where Member States mandate it via Art. 15 zones
  - Overridable in emergency situations

C0 and C4 drones: No geo-awareness/geofencing requirement
Privately built drones: No geo-awareness/geofencing requirement

step_by_step_guide.title: Understand U-Space and Future UAS Traffic Management
step_by_step_guide.description: Research the EU U-space regulatory framework (EU 2021/664, 2021/665, 2021/666). Understand the four phases of U-space deployment: U1 (e-registration, e-identification, geo-awareness), U2 (flight planning, tracking, airspace management), U3 (conflict management, capacity planning), and U4 (full integration with manned aviation). Identify U-space Service Providers (USSPs) operating in your country and explore how future drone flights will require filing digital flight plans and receiving automated clearances.
step_by_step_guide.code:
U-Space Deployment Phases:

U1 — Foundation Services:
  - E-registration of UAS operators
  - E-identification (remote ID)
  - Geo-awareness services

U2 — Initial Services:
  - Flight planning and approval
  - Tracking and monitoring
  - Airspace dynamic information
  - Weather information for UAS

U3 — Advanced Services:
  - Conflict detection and resolution
  - Capacity management
  - Tactical deconfliction

U4 — Full Integration:
  - Full integration with manned aviation ATM
  - Automated detect-and-avoid
  - Complex urban air mobility support

Key regulation: EU 2021/664 (U-space regulatory framework)

learning_script.title: Introduction to Airspace — Why It Matters for Drone Pilots
learning_script.content:
Welcome to your first lesson on airspace classification. If you are preparing to become a certified drone pilot in the European Union, understanding airspace is not just helpful — it is absolutely essential. Airspace is the invisible infrastructure that keeps all aviation safe, from commercial airliners cruising at 35,000 feet to your small quadcopter hovering at 50 metres above a field. Every cubic metre of sky above every country is classified, regulated, and — in many areas — actively managed by air traffic control.

As a drone pilot, you might wonder why airspace classification matters to you when you are flying a small unmanned aircraft at relatively low altitudes. The answer is simple: you share the sky with manned aircraft, and the consequences of a collision can be catastrophic. A drone weighing just 1 kilogram can cause severe damage to a helicopter windscreen or be ingested into a jet engine. Airspace rules exist to create a predictable, safe environment where all airspace users — manned and unmanned — can coexist.

The European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) has built its drone regulations on top of the existing airspace framework defined by the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO). When you study for your A1/A3 competency certificate, you will need to understand which airspace classes exist, where they apply, and what rules govern drone operations within each class. This knowledge directly affects where you can fly, what permissions you need, and how you plan safe and legal missions.

In this lesson, we will walk through the complete airspace classification system, from the most restrictive Class A airspace down to the uncontrolled Class G airspace where most drone operations occur. We will cover controlled airspace structures like CTRs and TMAs, the critical 120-metre height limit, UAS geographical zones, geofencing requirements, and the emerging U-space framework. By the end, you will have a solid foundation for the airspace-related questions on your A1/A3 exam.
key_takeaways: Airspace classification is fundamental knowledge for all drone pilots in the EU
Drones share the sky with manned aircraft, making airspace rules critical for safety
EASA drone regulations build upon the ICAO airspace classification framework
Understanding airspace directly determines where and how you can legally fly

learning_script.title: The ICAO Airspace Classification System (Classes A Through G)
learning_script.content:
The International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) is the United Nations agency responsible for establishing international standards for civil aviation. ICAO defines seven classes of airspace, designated by the letters A through G. Each class has specific rules about who may enter, what type of flight is permitted (instrument flight rules — IFR, or visual flight rules — VFR), whether air traffic control clearance is needed, and what separation services ATC provides.

At the top of the hierarchy is Class A airspace. This is the most restrictive class: only IFR flights are permitted, ATC clearance is mandatory, and full separation between all aircraft is provided by ATC. Class A is typically found in busy en-route airspace at higher altitudes. Drone operations in Class A airspace are effectively prohibited under the Open Category. Class B airspace allows both IFR and VFR flights but still requires ATC clearance and provides full separation. It is common around major international airports. Class C also allows IFR and VFR with ATC clearance, but provides separation only between IFR flights and between IFR and VFR flights — not between VFR flights themselves. Class D permits IFR and VFR with ATC clearance, providing IFR/IFR separation and traffic information to VFR flights.

Class E airspace is the transitional zone: IFR flights must have ATC clearance, but VFR flights may enter without clearance. ATC provides separation between IFR flights only. Many en-route airways in Europe are Class E. For drone pilots, Classes A through E are all considered controlled airspace, meaning that flying a drone in any of these classes requires prior authorisation — you cannot simply take off and fly.

Classes F and G are uncontrolled airspace. Class F provides an advisory service where available but no mandatory separation. Class G is the least restrictive: no ATC clearance is needed, and only flight information service is available. In practice, Class G extends from the surface up to the base of controlled airspace and is where the vast majority of Open Category drone operations take place. Understanding this hierarchy is essential because the moment your drone enters controlled airspace without authorisation, you are breaking the law and endangering other aircraft.
key_takeaways: ICAO defines 7 airspace classes (A-G) with decreasing levels of restriction
Classes A through E are controlled airspace requiring ATC clearance for drone operations
Classes F and G are uncontrolled airspace where most drone flights occur
Class G airspace extends from the surface upward and is the primary space for Open Category drones

learning_script.title: Controlled Airspace: CTR, TMA, and ATZ
learning_script.content:
Controlled airspace is not just an abstract classification — it takes the form of specific, geographically defined structures that protect critical areas of aviation activity. The three most important controlled airspace structures for drone pilots to understand are the Control Zone (CTR), the Terminal Manoeuvring Area (TMA), and the Aerodrome Traffic Zone (ATZ).

A Control Zone, or CTR, is a volume of controlled airspace that extends from the surface of the earth upward to a specified altitude, centred on one or more aerodromes. CTRs exist to protect aircraft during the most dangerous phases of flight: takeoff and landing. A typical CTR has a radius of 5 to 10 nautical miles around the airport and may extend vertically to 2,000 or 3,000 feet above ground level, though this varies. CTRs are usually classified as Class C or D airspace. For drone pilots, the critical point is that a CTR starts at the surface — meaning even a drone hovering at 10 metres altitude inside a CTR is in controlled airspace and requires prior authorisation. Many UAS geographical zones coincide with CTR boundaries.

Above and around the CTR, you will often find a Terminal Manoeuvring Area (TMA). The TMA is a volume of controlled airspace designed to protect aircraft transitioning between en-route flight and the approach/departure phases. Unlike a CTR, a TMA does not necessarily start at the surface — it has a defined lower limit (floor) which may be several thousand feet above ground level. For low-altitude drone operations, a TMA's floor may be well above your maximum altitude of 120 metres, meaning you could be below the TMA in uncontrolled Class G airspace while the TMA exists above you.

Aerodrome Traffic Zones (ATZ) are smaller protected areas around aerodromes, including those without a full CTR. An ATZ typically has a radius of 2 to 2.5 nautical miles and extends from the surface to 2,000 feet AGL. Even small grass airfields may have an ATZ. Many drone pilots make the dangerous mistake of assuming that only large commercial airports have protected airspace. In reality, small airfields, military installations, and heliports all have associated controlled or restricted airspace. Always check before you fly — the presence of a small airfield on your map means there is likely protected airspace around it.
key_takeaways: CTRs extend from the surface upward around airports — drones need authorisation at any altitude within them
TMAs sit above CTRs and may have a floor above typical drone operating altitudes
ATZs protect even small aerodromes and extend from the surface to approximately 2,000ft AGL
Never assume only large airports have protected airspace — always check your drone map before flying

learning_script.title: Uncontrolled Airspace: Class F and Class G
learning_script.content:
While controlled airspace gets much of the attention in aviation training, uncontrolled airspace is where the vast majority of drone operations actually take place. Understanding what 'uncontrolled' means — and what it does not mean — is crucial for safe drone operations.

Class F airspace is relatively uncommon in Europe. Where it exists, it provides an advisory service — meaning ATC can offer information and suggestions but does not provide mandatory separation between aircraft. Pilots in Class F airspace are responsible for their own separation. Some countries have phased out Class F entirely, reclassifying these areas as either Class E (controlled) or Class G (uncontrolled). As a drone pilot, you are unlikely to encounter Class F airspace in day-to-day operations, but you should be aware of its existence for your exam.

Class G airspace is where you will spend nearly all of your time as an Open Category drone pilot. Class G extends from the surface up to the base of any overlying controlled airspace. In areas far from airports, this might mean Class G extends from the ground up to a transition altitude of several thousand feet. Near airports, Class G may only exist up to a few hundred feet before you enter controlled airspace above. The key characteristic of Class G is that no ATC clearance is required to operate. However — and this is critically important — 'uncontrolled' does not mean 'unregulated'. All aviation rules still apply in Class G airspace, including the rules of the air, right-of-way rules, and drone-specific regulations under EU 2019/947.

In Class G airspace, the flight information service provides weather updates, NOTAMs (Notices to Air Missions), and other safety-relevant information, but it does not separate traffic. This means that other aircraft — helicopters, light aircraft, gliders, paragliders, and other drones — may be operating in the same airspace without any ATC coordination. As a drone pilot, you must maintain visual line of sight (VLOS) with your drone and actively scan for other air traffic. The responsibility for collision avoidance in uncontrolled airspace rests entirely with the pilots of each aircraft, including you as the remote pilot of a drone.

Another important consideration in Class G airspace is the presence of low-flying manned aircraft. Military fast jets, police helicopters, air ambulances, and agricultural aircraft all routinely operate at very low levels — sometimes below 500 feet AGL. These aircraft may appear suddenly and at high speed. This is one reason why the 120-metre AGL height limit exists: it creates a safety buffer between drone operations and the lowest levels at which most manned aircraft typically fly.
key_takeaways: Class G is the primary airspace for Open Category drone operations — no ATC clearance required
Uncontrolled does NOT mean unregulated — all aviation and drone rules still apply
Other aircraft operate in Class G without ATC coordination — pilots must self-separate
Low-flying manned aircraft (helicopters, military jets) routinely operate below 500ft AGL

learning_script.title: The 120-Metre AGL Rule and Vertical Limits for Drones
learning_script.content:
One of the most fundamental rules in EASA's Open Category is the maximum altitude limit: 120 metres above ground level (AGL). This rule, established in EU 2019/947, applies to all drone operations in the Open Category regardless of subcategory (A1, A2, or A3) or drone class (C0 through C4). Understanding exactly what this rule means — and its nuances — is essential for your A1/A3 exam and for safe operations.

First, let us clarify what 'above ground level' means. AGL measures altitude relative to the terrain directly below the drone, not sea level (AMSL — above mean sea level) and not the takeoff point. This distinction is critical when flying over varied terrain. If you take off from a hilltop that is 200 metres above the valley floor and fly over the valley, the ground level changes beneath you. Your drone must remain within 120 metres of the terrain at all times, which means if you fly over a valley, you might need to descend. Conversely, if you fly from a valley toward a hill, you will need to climb, but your altitude AGL must not exceed 120 metres at any point.

There is one notable exception to the 120-metre rule within the Open Category. When flying within a horizontal distance of 15 metres from an artificial obstacle taller than 105 metres (such as a telecommunications tower, wind turbine, or tall building), you may fly up to 15 metres above the top of that obstacle. This exception exists to allow inspection operations on tall structures. However, this exception requires the agreement of the entity responsible for the obstacle, and you must always respect any UAS geographical zone restrictions that may further limit your altitude.

Why 120 metres? This altitude was chosen to create a safety buffer between drone operations and the minimum safe altitude for most manned aircraft. In uncontrolled airspace, manned aircraft are generally expected to fly at or above 500 feet (approximately 150 metres) AGL over unpopulated areas. The 120-metre limit thus provides a 30-metre buffer. Over populated areas, manned aircraft fly even higher (1,000 feet minimum). While this buffer provides some safety margin, it is not absolute — military aircraft, emergency services, and others may fly lower. This is why visual scanning for other air traffic remains essential.

Your drone's barometric altimeter or GPS-reported altitude may not perfectly match your true AGL altitude, especially over uneven terrain. Always use the most conservative estimate of your altitude, and if in doubt, fly lower. Many drone apps and controllers display both GPS altitude and estimated AGL, but these can be inaccurate, particularly at distances from the takeoff point. Developing a good visual sense of altitude through practice is an invaluable skill.
key_takeaways: The 120m AGL limit applies to ALL Open Category operations, measured from the ground directly below
An exception allows flight up to 15m above artificial obstacles taller than 105m within 15m horizontal distance
The 120m limit creates a safety buffer below the 500ft minimum altitude for most manned aircraft
Always use the most conservative altitude estimate — altimeters can be inaccurate over varied terrain

learning_script.title: UAS Geographical Zones (Article 15, EU 2019/947)
learning_script.content:
Article 15 of EU 2019/947 grants each EU Member State the authority to designate UAS geographical zones — specific areas where drone operations are restricted, limited, or in some cases facilitated beyond the standard rules. This system recognises that aviation safety, security, privacy, and environmental concerns vary greatly from location to location, and a one-size-fits-all approach is insufficient. Understanding UAS geographical zones is vital because they directly determine where you can and cannot fly.

Member States may establish geographical zones for a variety of reasons. Common examples include zones around airports and aerodromes (often coinciding with CTRs), zones over government buildings, military installations, prisons, and critical infrastructure such as power plants and water treatment facilities. Environmental zones may protect nature reserves, national parks, or wildlife breeding areas from drone disturbance. Zones may also be established over densely populated urban areas to protect public safety and privacy. The restrictions within each zone can vary: some zones impose an absolute ban on all drone flights, others require specific authorisation, others limit operations to certain times or altitudes, and some may impose requirements for specific equipment like remote identification.

Importantly, UAS geographical zones can also facilitate drone operations. A Member State might designate a zone where the standard 120-metre height limit is raised, or where certain operational restrictions are relaxed. For example, dedicated drone testing zones or U-space airspace may have bespoke rules that allow more advanced operations. This two-way nature of geographical zones — both restricting and enabling — makes them a flexible regulatory tool.

As a drone pilot, you have a legal obligation to check for UAS geographical zones before every flight. Ignorance of a restriction is not a defence. Each Member State publishes its geographical zones through national channels — this might be a dedicated website, an app, or an amendment to the national Aeronautical Information Publication (AIP). The challenge for pilots is that there is no single EU-wide database yet, though efforts to harmonise zone publication are underway. If you plan to fly in another EU country, you must research that country's specific zone publication method.

The information published for each UAS geographical zone must include the zone's geographic boundaries (horizontal and vertical), the applicable conditions or restrictions, the time period during which the zone is active, the required drone equipage (if any), and the process for obtaining authorisation if applicable. Some zones are permanent while others are temporary — for instance, a zone established for a major sporting event or a visiting head of state. Temporary zones may be published as NOTAMs or through dedicated UAS zone notification channels.
key_takeaways: Article 15 of EU 2019/947 allows Member States to create UAS geographical zones that restrict or facilitate drone flights
Common restricted zones include airports, government buildings, prisons, critical infrastructure, and nature reserves
Drone pilots must check for UAS geographical zones before every flight — ignorance is not a legal defence
Zones can also facilitate operations by relaxing certain restrictions in designated areas

learning_script.title: Geofencing and Geo-Awareness for Class-Marked Drones
learning_script.content:
EU 2019/945 establishes technical requirements for class-marked drones (C0 through C4), including requirements for geo-awareness and geofencing systems. These systems are designed to reduce the risk of drones inadvertently entering restricted airspace or UAS geographical zones. Understanding the difference between geo-awareness and geofencing, and knowing which drone classes require each capability, is important for both the exam and practical operations.

Geo-awareness is a system that provides the remote pilot with information about airspace restrictions and UAS geographical zones. A drone with geo-awareness can load a database of geographical zone data, display this information to the pilot (typically through the controller app or screen), and generate warnings when the drone approaches or enters a restricted zone. The key point about geo-awareness is that it is informational — it warns the pilot but does not take any autonomous action to prevent the drone from entering the zone. The pilot retains full control and must decide whether to change course based on the warning. Geo-awareness is required for C1, C2, and C3 class drones.

Geofencing goes a step further than geo-awareness. A geofencing system actively prevents the drone from entering certain areas by imposing automated flight boundaries. When a drone with geofencing approaches a geofenced zone, the drone itself will refuse to enter that area — it may slow down, stop, or return, depending on the implementation. Geofencing is not automatically required for all class-marked drones; rather, EU 2019/945 requires that C1, C2, and C3 drones have the capability for geofencing, which can be activated when mandated by a UAS geographical zone established under Article 15. In other words, Member States decide where geofencing is required, and the drone must be able to comply.

C0 class drones (under 250 grams) and C4 class drones are not required to have geo-awareness or geofencing. Similarly, privately built drones used for recreational purposes under the Open Category are exempt from these requirements. However, the operational rules still apply — a pilot flying a C0 or privately built drone must still manually check for and comply with UAS geographical zones. The absence of an automated warning system places a greater responsibility on the pilot.

The geo-awareness database must be updatable by the pilot. This is important because UAS geographical zones change — new zones are created, existing zones are modified, and temporary zones come and go. If your drone's geo-awareness database is outdated, you might not receive a warning when approaching a newly established restricted zone. Regularly updating your drone's geographical zone data before flying is a critical pre-flight check. Many manufacturers provide automatic updates through companion apps, but the responsibility for ensuring the data is current rests with you as the pilot.
key_takeaways: Geo-awareness warns pilots about restricted zones; geofencing physically prevents entry into them
C1, C2, and C3 drones must have geo-awareness; geofencing capability is required but activated per Member State rules
C0 (<250g) and C4 drones are exempt from geo-awareness and geofencing requirements
Pilots must regularly update their drone's geo-awareness database before flying

learning_script.title: U-Space: The Future of UAS Traffic Management
learning_script.content:
As drone operations increase across Europe, the need for a comprehensive traffic management system for unmanned aircraft has become clear. The answer is U-space — a set of new services and procedures designed to ensure safe and efficient access to airspace for large numbers of drones, particularly in complex environments like urban areas and around airports. The regulatory foundation for U-space was established through three EU regulations adopted in 2021: EU 2021/664 (the U-space framework), EU 2021/665 (amendments to ATM/ANS rules for U-space), and EU 2021/666 (amendments to SERA — Standardised European Rules of the Air).

U-space is not a single system but rather an ecosystem of digital services provided by U-space Service Providers (USSPs). When U-space airspace is designated by a Member State, drone operators flying within that airspace must use the services of a certified USSP. These services include flight authorisation (submitting flight plans and receiving approval), geo-awareness (real-time information about airspace restrictions and other traffic), traffic information (knowing where other drones are operating), and weather information tailored to low-altitude UAS operations.

The concept envisions a phased deployment, often described as four maturity levels. U1 focuses on foundation services: e-registration of operators and drones, electronic identification (remote ID), and basic geo-awareness. U2 introduces initial operational services including flight planning, approval, tracking, and dynamic airspace information. U3 advances to more sophisticated services like automated conflict detection and resolution, tactical deconfliction, and capacity management. U4 represents the ultimate vision: full integration of unmanned and manned traffic management, enabling complex operations like urban air mobility with autonomous air taxis.

For you as an aspiring drone pilot, U-space has several practical implications. First, when flying in designated U-space airspace, you will need to submit a flight plan through a USSP and receive an authorisation before takeoff. Second, your drone will need to be equipped with electronic identification (remote ID) capability so that your flight can be tracked in real time. Third, you will receive real-time traffic information about other drones operating nearby, improving your situational awareness. While full U-space deployment is still evolving, early implementations are already operational in several European countries.

The designation of U-space airspace is the responsibility of each Member State. A Member State may designate certain areas — such as the airspace around an airport, over an urban area, or in a drone delivery corridor — as U-space airspace. When this happens, the baseline rules of the Open Category are supplemented by U-space requirements. This means that even in Class G airspace, if U-space is designated, you must comply with additional procedures. Staying informed about U-space developments in your country is part of being a responsible drone pilot.
key_takeaways: U-space is a set of digital services and regulations for managing drone traffic, established by EU 2021/664
U-space Service Providers (USSPs) deliver flight authorisation, tracking, geo-awareness, and traffic information
U-space airspace is designated by Member States and imposes additional requirements beyond standard Open Category rules
Remote identification (remote ID) is a key enabler for U-space operations

learning_script.title: Practical Airspace Assessment for Drone Flight Planning
learning_script.content:
Knowing airspace theory is important, but the real test of your competence is applying this knowledge to plan safe, legal drone flights. Every flight you undertake should begin with a systematic airspace assessment. This process becomes second nature with practice and is a cornerstone of professional drone operations. Let us walk through a practical assessment workflow that you should follow before every flight.

Start by determining the exact location and altitude of your planned operation. Open your national aviation authority's UAS geographical zone map and enter the coordinates or address of your planned flight area. Check for any restricted zones, prohibited zones, or zones with specific conditions. Note the vertical limits of any zones — some restrictions may only apply above certain altitudes. If your planned location falls within a restricted zone, determine what authorisation is required and whether you can obtain it in time, or choose an alternative location.

Next, check the airspace classification above your planned operating area. You can do this using a VFR aeronautical chart or a digital aviation mapping tool. Determine whether there is controlled airspace above you and, if so, what its lower limit (floor) is. If you are outside a CTR and the controlled airspace floor is above 120 metres AGL, you can operate in the Class G airspace below it without ATC authorisation. However, if you are within or near a CTR, the controlled airspace starts at the surface, and you must obtain authorisation before flying.

Check NOTAMs (Notices to Air Missions) for your area. NOTAMs can create temporary restrictions — for example, due to military exercises, major events, VIP movements, or emergency operations like firefighting or search and rescue. NOTAM checking is available through national aviation authority websites and various aviation apps. A NOTAM may restrict drone operations in areas that are normally available for flying, so this check must be performed on the day of your flight, not just during advance planning.

Consider the proximity of aerodromes, helipads, and other aviation infrastructure. Even if you are outside a CTR or ATZ, flying near an aerodrome increases the risk of encountering manned aircraft at low altitudes. Many national regulations establish specific distances from aerodromes within which drone operations are restricted or require notification. Check your country's specific rules regarding aerodrome proximity.

Finally, assess the terrain and any obstacles in your area. Tall structures, power lines, and elevated terrain can affect your true AGL altitude. If you are flying near a tall building or on sloping terrain, ensure that your drone stays within 120 metres of the ground at all points. Document your airspace assessment as part of your pre-flight checklist — this demonstrates professionalism and due diligence, and may be important if an incident occurs.
key_takeaways: Every drone flight must begin with a systematic airspace assessment including zone checks, airspace classification, and NOTAMs
Check UAS geographical zones, VFR charts, and active NOTAMs before every flight — conditions change daily
Consider aerodrome proximity even when outside formal CTR/ATZ boundaries
Document your airspace assessment as part of a pre-flight checklist for professional due diligence

learning_script.title: Summary and Key Concepts for the A1/A3 Exam
learning_script.content:
You have now covered all the essential airspace knowledge needed for the EASA A1/A3 competency certificate exam. Let us consolidate the key concepts and ensure you have a clear understanding of each topic area that may appear on the test.

Airspace classification is the foundation. There are seven ICAO classes — A through G. Classes A through E are controlled airspace where ATC provides varying levels of service and clearance is required. Classes F and G are uncontrolled. For Open Category drone pilots, Class G is your primary operating environment. Flying in controlled airspace always requires prior authorisation from the competent authority or ATC. Remember the specific controlled airspace structures: CTRs extend from the surface around airports, TMAs sit above them, and ATZs protect the immediate vicinity of aerodromes.

The 120-metre AGL rule is absolute within the Open Category, with only one exception: you may fly up to 15 metres above an artificial obstacle taller than 105 metres, provided you are within 15 metres horizontal distance of that obstacle and have the agreement of the obstacle's responsible entity. AGL means above the ground directly below the drone, not above sea level or above the takeoff point. This distinction is critical over varied terrain.

UAS geographical zones, established under Article 15 of EU 2019/947, are the primary mechanism through which Member States tailor drone restrictions to local conditions. These zones can prohibit, restrict, or facilitate drone operations. You must check for them before every flight using your national authority's published information. Class-marked drones C1, C2, and C3 must have geo-awareness systems that can load and display zone information. Geofencing capability — which physically prevents the drone from entering restricted areas — must be available on C1, C2, and C3 drones and can be mandated by Member States for specific zones. C0 and C4 class drones and privately built drones are exempt from these equipment requirements, but the pilots must still comply with all zone restrictions manually.

U-space is the future of UAS traffic management in Europe, governed by EU 2021/664 and supporting regulations. It will provide digital services through USSPs, including flight authorisation, tracking, traffic information, and conflict management. When U-space airspace is designated by a Member State, additional requirements apply beyond the standard Open Category rules. Understanding U-space concepts shows examiners that you are aware of the evolving regulatory landscape.

For the exam, ensure you can distinguish between controlled and uncontrolled airspace, name the airspace classes, explain the 120m AGL rule and its exception, describe the purpose of UAS geographical zones and the pilot's obligation to check them, explain the difference between geo-awareness and geofencing, and outline the basics of U-space. Practice with sample questions, review the key regulations (EU 2019/947 and EU 2019/945), and always think about how these rules apply in practical flight planning scenarios. Good luck with your studies, and fly safe!
key_takeaways: Master the 7 ICAO airspace classes, controlled vs uncontrolled distinction, and the 120m AGL rule with its exception
Know the pilot's obligation to check UAS geographical zones before every flight under Article 15
Understand the difference between geo-awareness (warnings) and geofencing (physical prevention) for class-marked drones
Be familiar with U-space concepts and the phased deployment approach for UAS traffic management

lessons.title: Weather & Meteorology
lessons.detailed_explanation:
Meteorology is arguably the single most important external factor affecting safe drone operations. Unlike manned aircraft, small unmanned aerial systems (sUAS) are highly susceptible to weather conditions due to their low mass, limited power reserves, and operational altitude in the atmospheric boundary layer where weather effects are most pronounced. Under EASA regulations for the A1/A3 open category, remote pilots must be competent in assessing weather conditions before and during every flight. A failure to properly evaluate meteorological factors is a leading contributor to drone incidents across Europe, ranging from loss of control in unexpected gusts to flyaways caused by high-altitude winds that differ significantly from ground-level conditions.

Wind is the primary weather concern for multirotor drone pilots. Most consumer and prosumer drones have maximum wind resistance ratings between 8 and 12 m/s (approximately Beaufort Force 4-5). However, safe operational limits are typically lower, as sustained flight into strong headwinds dramatically reduces battery endurance and ground speed. The Beaufort scale provides a practical framework for estimating wind speed based on observable environmental effects — for example, Force 4 (moderate breeze, 5.5–7.9 m/s) causes small branches to move and raises dust, while Force 6 (strong breeze, 10.8–13.8 m/s) makes umbrella use difficult and should be considered a no-fly condition for most small drones. Wind gusts are particularly dangerous because they represent sudden, short-duration increases in wind speed that can exceed a drone's ability to compensate, leading to attitude disturbances, positional drift, or even structural failure in extreme cases.

Cloud types provide essential visual cues about current and developing weather conditions. Cumulus clouds indicate thermal convection and can signal turbulent air at low altitudes. Towering cumulus and cumulonimbus clouds are associated with thunderstorms, severe turbulence, wind shear, microbursts, heavy precipitation, and even hail — all extremely hazardous to drone operations. Stratus clouds are layered and uniform, often associated with stable air and reduced visibility. Fog, which is essentially a cloud at ground level, can reduce visibility below the minimums required for Visual Line of Sight (VLOS) operations. Under EASA regulations, VLOS operations require the remote pilot to maintain continuous unaided visual contact with the UA, which generally requires visibility of at least 1-2 km depending on the drone's size and the operational scenario. Understanding visibility requirements, cloud base heights, and their implications for maximum flight altitude is fundamental to regulatory compliance.

Temperature significantly impacts lithium polymer (LiPo) battery chemistry. Below approximately 10°C, internal resistance in LiPo cells increases, reducing available capacity and maximum discharge current. At temperatures below 0°C, capacity can drop by 20-40%, dramatically reducing flight time and increasing the risk of unexpected voltage sag under load. Conversely, high temperatures above 40°C accelerate battery degradation and increase the risk of thermal runaway. Atmospheric pressure and density altitude also affect UAS performance: at higher density altitudes (caused by high temperature, low pressure, or high humidity), air density decreases, requiring the motors to spin faster to generate the same thrust, which increases power consumption and reduces flight time. Drone pilots should also understand how to read METARs (Meteorological Aerodrome Reports) and TAFs (Terminal Aerodrome Forecasts) — standardised aviation weather reports that provide coded information about wind, visibility, clouds, temperature, and pressure at aerodromes. While drone pilots may not always operate near aerodromes, METARs and TAFs remain the most reliable and standardised source of local weather data available to aviators.

step_by_step_guide.title: Understanding Atmospheric Layers and Weather Formation
step_by_step_guide.description: The troposphere is the lowest layer of the atmosphere, extending from the surface to approximately 8-15 km altitude (lower at the poles, higher at the equator). Nearly all weather phenomena occur in the troposphere because it contains the majority of atmospheric water vapour and experiences convective mixing driven by surface heating. Temperature generally decreases with altitude at an average rate of approximately 6.5°C per 1,000 metres (the environmental lapse rate). Understanding this structure helps drone pilots predict how conditions at their operating altitude may differ from ground-level observations. The tropopause marks the boundary where temperature stops decreasing, and above it lies the stratosphere. For UAS operations in the open category (limited to 120 m AGL), pilots operate well within the boundary layer — the lowest portion of the troposphere directly influenced by the Earth's surface, where friction, thermal convection, and terrain effects create the most variable and turbulent conditions.

step_by_step_guide.title: Assess Wind Conditions Using the Beaufort Scale and Anemometer
step_by_step_guide.description: Before every flight, assess wind speed using a handheld anemometer and cross-reference with observable indicators from the Beaufort scale. Record the sustained wind speed and note gusts. Compare readings to your drone's maximum wind resistance specification and apply a safety margin — a good rule of thumb is to limit operations to no more than two-thirds of the drone's rated wind resistance. Remember that wind speed increases with altitude and can be significantly stronger at 100 m AGL than at ground level. Also assess wind direction relative to your planned flight path, as persistent headwinds will reduce ground speed and increase battery consumption on the outbound leg.
step_by_step_guide.code:
BEAUFORT SCALE REFERENCE (Drone-Relevant Excerpts):

Force | Speed (m/s) | Description        | Observable Effects             | Drone Impact
------|-------------|--------------------|---------------------------------|------------------
  0   |  0 - 0.2   | Calm               | Smoke rises vertically          | Ideal conditions
  1   | 0.3 - 1.5  | Light air           | Smoke drifts slowly             | Ideal conditions
  2   | 1.6 - 3.3  | Light breeze        | Leaves rustle, wind felt        | Good conditions
  3   | 3.4 - 5.4  | Gentle breeze       | Leaves/twigs in motion          | Good, minor drift
  4   | 5.5 - 7.9  | Moderate breeze     | Small branches move, dust rises | Caution, reduced endurance
  5   | 8.0 - 10.7 | Fresh breeze        | Small trees sway                | Limit for most drones
  6   | 10.8 - 13.8| Strong breeze       | Large branches move, umbrellas  | NO-GO for most sUAS
  7   | 13.9 - 17.1| Near gale           | Whole trees in motion           | NO-GO — Grounded

step_by_step_guide.title: Identify Cloud Types and Their Implications for Flight Safety
step_by_step_guide.description: Learn to identify the main cloud types by altitude and form. High clouds (cirrus, cirrostratus, cirrocumulus) above 6,000 m generally do not directly affect drone operations but can indicate approaching weather fronts. Mid-level clouds (altostratus, altocumulus) between 2,000-6,000 m may signal incoming precipitation. Low-level clouds (stratus, stratocumulus, nimbostratus) below 2,000 m can reduce ceiling heights and visibility. Vertically developing clouds are the most critical to monitor: cumulus congestus (towering cumulus) and cumulonimbus indicate strong convective activity, potential thunderstorms, severe turbulence, and dangerous downdrafts. The presence of cumulonimbus clouds within 30 km of your operating area should trigger an automatic no-go decision.
step_by_step_guide.code:
CLOUD TYPE QUICK REFERENCE FOR DRONE PILOTS:

High (>6 km):    Cirrus (Ci) — thin, wispy, ice crystals
                 Cirrostratus (Cs) — thin veil, halo around sun
                 Cirrocumulus (Cc) — small white patches

Mid (2-6 km):    Altostratus (As) — grey sheet, sun dimly visible
                 Altocumulus (Ac) — white/grey patches in rows

Low (<2 km):     Stratus (St) — uniform grey layer, drizzle
                 Stratocumulus (Sc) — lumpy grey layer
                 Nimbostratus (Ns) — thick, dark, continuous rain

Vertical:        Cumulus (Cu) — fair weather, puffy white
                 Cumulus congestus — towering, growing vertically
                 Cumulonimbus (Cb) — THUNDERSTORM CLOUD
                   ⚠ Turbulence, wind shear, microbursts, hail, lightning

step_by_step_guide.title: Decode a METAR Weather Report
step_by_step_guide.description: A METAR (Meteorological Aerodrome Report) is an hourly weather observation issued at aerodromes worldwide using a standardised ICAO format. Learn to decode each element: station identifier, date/time group, wind direction and speed, visibility, weather phenomena, cloud layers, temperature/dew point, and pressure (QNH). METARs use UTC time (denoted by 'Z' for Zulu). Wind is reported as direction in degrees true and speed in knots. Visibility is reported in metres (or statute miles in the USA). Cloud cover uses standard abbreviations: FEW (1-2 oktas), SCT (3-4 oktas), BKN (5-7 oktas), OVC (8 oktas). Practice decoding real METARs from your local aerodrome to build proficiency.
step_by_step_guide.code:
EXAMPLE METAR:
METAR EDDF 151350Z 27012G22KT 9999 FEW040 SCT080 18/08 Q1013 NOSIG

DECODED:
  EDDF        — Station: Frankfurt Airport (ICAO code)
  151350Z     — 15th of the month, 13:50 UTC (Zulu)
  27012G22KT  — Wind from 270° (west) at 12 knots, gusting 22 knots
  9999        — Visibility: 10 km or more (excellent)
  FEW040      — Few clouds at 4,000 ft AGL (1-2 oktas)
  SCT080      — Scattered clouds at 8,000 ft AGL (3-4 oktas)
  18/08       — Temperature 18°C, Dew point 8°C
  Q1013       — QNH pressure 1013 hPa
  NOSIG       — No significant change expected

⚠ DRONE PILOT CHECK:
  Wind: 12 kt (≈6.2 m/s) sustained, gusts 22 kt (≈11.3 m/s)
  → Gusts may exceed safe limits for lighter drones!
  Visibility: Excellent for VLOS
  Clouds: Well above 120 m AGL ceiling — no issue

step_by_step_guide.title: Decode a TAF Weather Forecast
step_by_step_guide.description: A TAF (Terminal Aerodrome Forecast) provides a weather forecast for an aerodrome and surrounding area, typically valid for 24 or 30 hours. TAFs use the same coding as METARs but include forecast change groups: BECMG (becoming — gradual change), TEMPO (temporary fluctuations), and PROB30/PROB40 (probability of occurrence). TAFs are issued four times daily and are essential for planning drone operations hours in advance. The validity period, wind, visibility, cloud, and significant weather are all encoded. Understanding TAFs allows you to anticipate deteriorating conditions and plan your flight window accordingly.
step_by_step_guide.code:
EXAMPLE TAF:
TAF EHAM 150500Z 1506/1612 24015KT 9999 SCT035
  BECMG 1510/1512 28020G30KT 5000 RA BKN015
  TEMPO 1512/1518 3000 +RA BKN008
  BECMG 1518/1520 25012KT 9999 SCT040

DECODED:
  EHAM            — Station: Amsterdam Schiphol
  150500Z         — Issued 15th at 05:00 UTC
  1506/1612       — Valid from 15th 06:00 to 16th 12:00 UTC
  24015KT 9999 SCT035 — Base forecast: Wind 240°/15kt, >10km vis, scattered at 3500ft

  BECMG 1510/1512 — Between 10:00-12:00, becoming:
    28020G30KT    — Wind shifting to 280° at 20kt gusting 30kt
    5000 RA       — Visibility dropping to 5km in rain
    BKN015        — Broken cloud base at 1500ft

  TEMPO 1512/1518 — Temporarily between 12:00-18:00:
    3000 +RA BKN008 — 3km vis, heavy rain, broken clouds at 800ft

  BECMG 1518/1520 — Between 18:00-20:00, improving:
    25012KT 9999 SCT040 — Moderate wind, good visibility

⚠ DRONE PILOT: Plan flights for morning (06-10Z) or evening (after 20Z).
  Midday period is NO-GO: gusts 30kt, low visibility, heavy rain.

step_by_step_guide.title: Evaluate Visibility and Cloud Base for VLOS Compliance
step_by_step_guide.description: For VLOS drone operations under EASA open category rules, you must maintain continuous unaided visual contact with your drone at all times. This practically requires a minimum visibility of approximately 1-2 km, though specific requirements may vary by national implementation. The cloud base (ceiling) is critical because you must not fly into clouds or above them — the maximum altitude of 120 m AGL in the open category means a cloud base below approximately 150 m (roughly 500 ft) could be problematic, and fog (visibility below 1,000 m) makes VLOS operations effectively impossible. Use the METAR visibility and cloud fields to assess compliance. The difference between temperature and dew point (the spread) indicates fog risk: when the spread narrows below 3°C, fog formation becomes increasingly likely, especially around dawn and in valleys or near bodies of water.
step_by_step_guide.code:
VLOS VISIBILITY DECISION MATRIX:

Visibility (METAR) | VLOS Assessment       | Action
-------------------|-----------------------|---------------------------
  > 5,000 m        | Good                  | VLOS operations OK
  3,000 - 5,000 m  | Marginal              | Reduce operating range
  1,000 - 3,000 m  | Poor                  | Caution — close range only
  < 1,000 m         | Below VLOS minimums   | NO-GO

CLOUD BASE CHECK:
  Cloud base > 500 ft AGL  → OK for 120 m max altitude
  Cloud base 300-500 ft AGL → Reduce max altitude
  Cloud base < 300 ft AGL  → NO-GO (risk of cloud entry)

FOG RISK — Temperature/Dew Point Spread:
  Spread > 5°C   → Low fog risk
  Spread 3-5°C   → Moderate — monitor closely
  Spread < 3°C   → High risk — fog likely forming
  Spread 0-1°C   → Fog imminent or present

step_by_step_guide.title: Make a Go/No-Go Weather Decision for a Drone Flight
step_by_step_guide.description: Combine all weather assessment factors into a structured go/no-go decision. Before every flight, gather current METAR data (or on-site observations), check the TAF for expected changes during your flight window, assess wind speed and gusts against your drone's limits with a safety margin, confirm visibility meets VLOS requirements, check cloud base is above your maximum intended altitude, verify temperature is within your battery's operating range, and scan for hazardous weather phenomena (Cb clouds, frontal passages, precipitation). Document your weather assessment as part of your pre-flight checklist. If any single factor is marginal, apply extra caution. If any factor is in the no-go range, do not fly. Weather can change rapidly — continue monitoring throughout the flight and be prepared to land immediately if conditions deteriorate.
step_by_step_guide.code:
PRE-FLIGHT WEATHER CHECKLIST:

□ 1. WIND
     Sustained: _____ m/s  (Limit: 2/3 of drone max = _____ m/s)
     Gusts:     _____ m/s  (Limit: drone max = _____ m/s)
     → GO / NO-GO

□ 2. VISIBILITY
     Reported: _____ m     (Minimum: 1000-2000 m for VLOS)
     → GO / NO-GO

□ 3. CLOUD BASE
     Lowest layer: _____ ft AGL  (Minimum: ~500 ft for 120m ops)
     → GO / NO-GO

□ 4. PRECIPITATION
     Current: None / Rain / Snow / Hail
     → GO / NO-GO (Rain = caution, Hail/Snow = NO-GO)

□ 5. TEMPERATURE
     Current: _____ °C  (Battery range: 0°C to 40°C ideal)
     → GO / NO-GO

□ 6. HAZARDOUS PHENOMENA
     Thunderstorms within 30 km?    Y / N
     Wind shear or microburst risk? Y / N
     Fog forming or expected?       Y / N
     → Any YES = NO-GO

□ 7. FORECAST TREND (TAF)
     Conditions improving / stable / deteriorating?
     → Deteriorating = shorten flight or NO-GO

FINAL DECISION:  □ GO  □ NO-GO
Reason: _________________________________

quiz.question: In the following METAR excerpt '27012G22KT', what does 'G22' indicate?
options: Ground visibility is 22 km
Wind gusts up to 22 knots
Cloud base at 2,200 feet
Temperature is 22°C
quiz.answer: Wind gusts up to 22 knots

quiz.question: What is the maximum recommended wind speed for most consumer multirotor drones?
options: 5 m/s (Beaufort Force 3)
8-10 m/s (Beaufort Force 5)
15 m/s (Beaufort Force 7)
20 m/s (Beaufort Force 8)
quiz.answer: 8-10 m/s (Beaufort Force 5)

quiz.question: Which cloud type is most strongly associated with thunderstorms, severe turbulence, and microburst risk?
options: Cirrostratus
Altostratus
Stratus
Cumulonimbus
quiz.answer: Cumulonimbus

quiz.question: What is the minimum visibility generally required for VLOS drone operations?
options: 500 metres
1,000 metres
5,000 metres
10,000 metres
quiz.answer: 1,000 metres

quiz.question: How does cold temperature (below 10°C) primarily affect LiPo drone batteries?
options: It increases battery capacity and extends flight time
It increases internal resistance, reducing capacity and discharge rate
It has no measurable effect on modern LiPo cells
It only affects battery charging, not discharge performance
quiz.answer: It increases internal resistance, reducing capacity and discharge rate

quiz.question: In a METAR, the cloud abbreviation 'BKN025' means:
options: Broken cloud cover at 2,500 feet AGL (5-7 oktas)
Below known ceiling at 25 metres
Broken cloud cover at 250 feet AGL (3-4 oktas)
Bank of fog at 2,500 metres visibility
quiz.answer: Broken cloud cover at 2,500 feet AGL (5-7 oktas)

quiz.question: A microburst is dangerous to drones because it:
options: Creates a localised column of rapidly descending air that can exceed a drone's climb rate
Generates constant steady wind that slowly pushes the drone off course
Only occurs at altitudes above 500 metres, well above drone operations
Produces electromagnetic interference that disrupts GPS signals
quiz.answer: Creates a localised column of rapidly descending air that can exceed a drone's climb rate

quiz.question: What does a narrowing temperature/dew point spread (less than 3°C difference) in a METAR indicate?
options: Decreasing wind speed expected
Increasing risk of fog or mist formation
Improving visibility conditions
Rising atmospheric pressure
quiz.answer: Increasing risk of fog or mist formation

quiz.question: In a TAF, the change group 'TEMPO 1512/1518 3000 +RA BKN008' means:
options: Permanently from 15:12 to 15:18, visibility 3 km in heavy rain with broken clouds at 800 ft
Temporarily between 12:00 and 18:00 UTC on the 15th, visibility 3,000 m in heavy rain with broken clouds at 800 ft
Temperature dropping 15°C to 12°C between 18:00 and 30:00 UTC
Temporary restriction area 3,000 ft radius with radio antenna at 800 ft
quiz.answer: Temporarily between 12:00 and 18:00 UTC on the 15th, visibility 3,000 m in heavy rain with broken clouds at 800 ft

quiz.question: How does high density altitude (caused by high temperature, low pressure, or high humidity) affect multirotor drone performance?
options: It improves motor efficiency due to reduced air friction
It has no effect because drones fly at very low altitudes
It reduces air density, requiring motors to work harder for the same thrust, increasing power consumption
It only affects fixed-wing drones, not multirotors
quiz.answer: It reduces air density, requiring motors to work harder for the same thrust, increasing power consumption

learning_script.title: Introduction: Why Weather Is a Drone Pilot's Most Important Variable
learning_script.content:
Of all the factors that influence the safety and success of a drone flight, weather stands alone as the most dynamic, unpredictable, and potentially dangerous. Unlike airspace regulations that remain relatively stable, or equipment that can be inspected and maintained, weather conditions can change rapidly and without warning, transforming a safe flying environment into a hazardous one in minutes. For small unmanned aerial systems, the stakes are even higher than for manned aircraft: a drone weighing a few kilograms has far less inertia to resist gusts, far less power reserve to fight headwinds, and far less structural strength to withstand turbulence.

The European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) recognises the critical importance of meteorological knowledge for drone pilots. The A1/A3 competency certificate examination includes questions on weather assessment, and the regulatory framework places the responsibility for weather-related go/no-go decisions squarely on the remote pilot. There is no air traffic controller managing your drone's route, no dispatcher monitoring conditions on your behalf — you alone must gather, interpret, and act on weather information before and during every flight.

This lesson will equip you with the meteorological knowledge you need to fly safely and legally in the European open category. You will learn to understand how the atmosphere creates weather, identify cloud types and what they signal, assess wind and visibility conditions, interpret standardised aviation weather reports (METARs and TAFs), and make informed go/no-go decisions. Whether you fly for recreation, photography, surveying, or inspection, this knowledge will help you avoid the weather-related incidents that remain one of the leading causes of drone accidents across Europe.

Think of weather assessment not as a bureaucratic requirement but as a core piloting skill — one that separates competent pilots from those who simply press the takeoff button and hope for the best. Every professional drone operation begins with a thorough weather briefing, and by the end of this lesson, you will have the tools and knowledge to conduct one yourself.
key_takeaways: Weather is the most dynamic and dangerous variable in drone operations, capable of changing rapidly
EASA places full responsibility for weather assessment on the remote pilot — there is no ATC support for drone flights
Meteorological competence is tested in the A1/A3 exam and is a legal obligation under EU regulations
Proper weather assessment is a core piloting skill, not just a regulatory checkbox

learning_script.title: The Atmosphere: Layers, Pressure, and Temperature
learning_script.content:
To understand weather, you first need to understand the medium in which your drone flies: the atmosphere. The Earth's atmosphere is divided into distinct layers, each with different characteristics. The troposphere is the lowest layer, extending from the surface to approximately 8 km at the poles and 15 km at the equator. This is where virtually all weather occurs, and it is the only atmospheric layer relevant to drone operations. Within the troposphere, temperature generally decreases with altitude at an average rate of about 6.5°C per 1,000 metres — this is called the environmental lapse rate.

Atmospheric pressure is the weight of the column of air above a given point. At sea level, standard atmospheric pressure is 1013.25 hPa (hectopascals). Pressure decreases with altitude because there is less air above you as you go higher. This pressure is reported in METARs as the QNH value, which allows altimeters to be set correctly. For drone pilots, atmospheric pressure matters because it affects air density and therefore the aerodynamic performance of your rotors.

Density altitude is a critical concept that combines the effects of pressure, temperature, and humidity into a single number representing the altitude at which the current air density would be found in the International Standard Atmosphere. On a hot, low-pressure, humid day, the density altitude can be significantly higher than the actual elevation — meaning the air behaves as if you were flying at a much higher altitude. For a multirotor drone, this means the propellers must spin faster to generate the same lift, consuming more battery power and reducing flight time. At extreme density altitudes, a drone may not be able to take off at all or may struggle to maintain altitude with a full payload.

The atmospheric boundary layer is the lowest portion of the troposphere, typically extending from the surface to 500-2,000 metres depending on conditions. This layer is directly influenced by the Earth's surface through friction, heating, and terrain effects. It is characterised by turbulence, wind shear, and rapid temperature changes — all of which directly affect drone operations. During the day, solar heating creates convective thermals that rise through the boundary layer, causing turbulent, bumpy conditions. At night, the boundary layer often becomes stable, with calm conditions near the surface but potentially strong winds just a few hundred metres above.
key_takeaways: Nearly all weather occurs in the troposphere; temperature decreases about 6.5°C per 1,000 m altitude
Density altitude combines pressure, temperature, and humidity effects — high density altitude reduces drone performance
The atmospheric boundary layer (0-2,000 m) is where drones operate and where turbulence and wind variability are greatest

learning_script.title: Wind: Types, Measurement, and Effects on Multirotors
learning_script.content:
Wind is the horizontal movement of air caused by differences in atmospheric pressure between locations. Air flows from high-pressure areas to low-pressure areas, modified by the Coriolis effect due to Earth's rotation and by friction with the surface. For drone pilots, understanding wind is essential because it directly determines whether a flight is safe, how long the battery will last, and how much control authority the pilot has over the aircraft.

Wind is described by two properties: direction (where it comes from, in degrees true) and speed (typically in knots for aviation or metres per second for general use). The conversion is straightforward: 1 knot equals approximately 0.514 m/s. Wind speed at the surface is always lower than at altitude due to friction with terrain and obstacles — this difference is called wind shear. As a general rule, wind speed at 100 metres above ground can be 1.5 to 2 times the surface wind speed, meaning that a manageable 5 m/s breeze at ground level could be 8-10 m/s at your drone's operating altitude.

The Beaufort scale provides a practical system for estimating wind speed based on observable effects in the environment. Force 0-3 (calm to gentle breeze, 0-5.4 m/s) represents ideal to good conditions for most drones. Force 4 (moderate breeze, 5.5-7.9 m/s) requires caution as battery endurance will be noticeably reduced. Force 5 (fresh breeze, 8.0-10.7 m/s) approaches the limits of many consumer drones and should only be attempted with high-performance aircraft. Force 6 and above should be considered no-go conditions for all but the most robust industrial drones.

Gusts are sudden, brief increases in wind speed that are particularly hazardous because they can exceed a drone's maximum attitude correction rate, causing momentary loss of control. METARs report gusts when the peak speed exceeds the average by 10 or more knots. Turbulence — chaotic, irregular air movement — is caused by mechanical effects (wind flowing over obstacles like buildings, trees, and terrain) and thermal effects (convective updrafts and downdrafts). Mechanical turbulence is most severe on the downwind side of obstacles, in an area called the turbulence zone, which extends downwind for a distance of 10-20 times the obstacle's height. Thermal turbulence is strongest on sunny afternoons over dark surfaces that absorb heat. Both types create unpredictable forces on a drone that degrade GPS hold accuracy and can cause altitude and position deviations.
key_takeaways: Wind at 100 m altitude can be 1.5-2x stronger than at ground level due to wind shear
Beaufort Force 5 (8-10.7 m/s) is typically the upper limit for consumer drone operations
Gusts are more dangerous than steady wind because they can exceed a drone's correction rate
Mechanical turbulence extends 10-20x the height of an obstacle on its downwind side

learning_script.title: Cloud Classification and What Each Type Means for Flight
learning_script.content:
Clouds are visible masses of water droplets or ice crystals suspended in the atmosphere. For a drone pilot, clouds serve as essential visual indicators of atmospheric conditions, stability, and potential hazards. The international cloud classification system organises clouds by their altitude and form, and learning to identify the major types gives you valuable real-time information about the weather without any instruments.

High-level clouds form above 6,000 metres and include cirrus (thin, wispy streaks of ice crystals), cirrostratus (thin veils that create halos around the sun or moon), and cirrocumulus (small, white, grain-like patches). These clouds do not directly affect drone operations at 120 m AGL, but they can indicate approaching weather systems. A progression from cirrus to cirrostratus to lower clouds often signals an approaching warm front with deteriorating conditions within 12-24 hours.

Mid-level clouds between 2,000 and 6,000 metres include altostratus (grey, featureless sheets through which the sun appears as if through frosted glass) and altocumulus (white or grey patches arranged in rows or waves). Altocumulus castellanus — mid-level clouds with tower-like protrusions — is particularly significant because it indicates instability in the mid-atmosphere and can be a precursor to afternoon thunderstorms. Seeing these in the morning should alert you to potential convective weather later in the day.

Low-level clouds below 2,000 metres are the most operationally significant for drone pilots. Stratus is a uniform, grey, featureless layer that can produce drizzle and significantly reduce visibility. Stratocumulus appears as a lumpy, rolling grey layer — it is the most common cloud type globally and generally indicates stable conditions with limited vertical development. Nimbostratus is a thick, dark, rain-bearing layer that produces continuous moderate precipitation — a clear no-go indicator.

Vertically developing clouds deserve special attention. Fair-weather cumulus (small, white, puffy clouds with flat bases) indicates thermal convection and generally benign conditions, though the thermals producing them can cause mild turbulence. When cumulus clouds grow significantly taller than they are wide, they become cumulus congestus (towering cumulus), indicating strong updrafts and increasing instability. The final stage is the cumulonimbus — the thunderstorm cloud — characterised by its enormous vertical extent (often reaching the tropopause), anvil-shaped top, and dark base. Cumulonimbus clouds produce lightning, heavy rain, hail, severe turbulence, and microbursts, and represent an absolute no-fly condition for any drone operation.
key_takeaways: High clouds (cirrus family) can signal approaching fronts; mid-level castellanus warns of afternoon thunderstorms
Low clouds (stratus, nimbostratus) reduce visibility and ceiling, directly affecting VLOS operations
Cumulonimbus is the thunderstorm cloud — an absolute no-go for all drone operations
Fair-weather cumulus indicates thermals and mild turbulence at low altitudes

learning_script.title: Visibility, Fog, and Precipitation — VLOS Implications
learning_script.content:
Visibility is the greatest distance at which a prominent object can be seen and identified with the unaided eye. For VLOS drone operations, visibility determines whether you can maintain the continuous visual contact with your aircraft that regulations require. Under EASA open category rules, the remote pilot must be able to see the drone well enough to determine its position, altitude, and direction of flight at all times. While EASA does not specify an exact minimum visibility figure for the open category, practical experience and national guidelines typically suggest a minimum of 1-2 km, depending on the drone's size and the operating distance.

Fog is the most common visibility-reducing phenomenon and is defined as a suspension of very small water droplets in the air that reduces visibility below 1,000 metres. Mist is similar but with visibility between 1,000 and 5,000 metres. Radiation fog forms on clear, calm nights when the ground cools by radiating heat, cooling the adjacent air below its dew point. It is most common in valleys, near rivers, and in autumn and winter. Advection fog forms when warm, moist air moves over a cooler surface — common in coastal areas when sea breezes bring moist maritime air over cooler land. Fog typically forms when the temperature/dew point spread narrows below 2-3°C, and monitoring this spread in METARs gives early warning of potential fog formation.

Precipitation in any form degrades visibility and can directly damage or impair drone equipment. Rain reduces visibility, can enter motor housings and electronics enclosures, interferes with optical sensors and cameras, and adds weight to the airframe. Most consumer drones have an IP rating of IP43 or lower, meaning they are not designed for operation in rain. Snow and sleet create similar visibility and moisture problems, with the added risk of ice accumulation on propellers that degrades lift and increases vibration. Hail, even small hailstones, can physically damage propellers, gimbal mechanisms, and camera lenses, and is often accompanied by severe turbulence and strong downdrafts associated with cumulonimbus clouds.

When assessing visibility for a drone flight, consider not only the reported visibility in METARs (which is measured horizontally at aerodrome level) but also factors specific to your location. Terrain, industrial emissions, agricultural dust, pollen, and wildfire smoke can all reduce local visibility below reported aerodrome values. The sun's position matters too — looking towards the sun (backlit conditions) makes it much harder to see a small drone at distance. Always plan your operating position so that you face away from the sun when possible, and be especially cautious during dawn and dusk when contrast is reduced.
key_takeaways: VLOS requires continuous unaided visual contact; practically this needs 1-2 km minimum visibility
Fog forms when the temperature/dew point spread drops below 2-3°C — monitor this in METARs
Most consumer drones are not waterproof (IP43 or below); rain, snow, and hail are operational hazards
Local visibility can differ significantly from aerodrome METAR values due to terrain, pollution, and sun glare

learning_script.title: Temperature Extremes: Battery Performance and Airframe Stress
learning_script.content:
Temperature is one of the most underestimated weather factors in drone operations, yet it has profound effects on both the aircraft's power system and its structural components. Lithium polymer (LiPo) batteries, which power the vast majority of consumer and professional drones, are chemical devices whose performance is highly temperature-dependent. Understanding this relationship is essential for safe flight planning and avoiding the sudden power loss that has caused numerous drone incidents.

In cold conditions below 10°C, the electrochemical reactions within LiPo cells slow down, increasing internal resistance. This manifests as a reduction in available capacity (the battery appears to drain faster) and a reduction in maximum discharge current (the battery cannot deliver peak power when demanded).
