The Q-line uses coded qualifiers such as the FIR identifier, NOTAM code (subject and condition), traffic type, purpose, scope, and geographical coordinates with a radius. Learning to decode these fields is essential for any drone pilot, as it allows rapid assessment of whether a NOTAM affects a planned flight area and altitude.

Drone pilots in Europe can access NOTAMs through several channels. National Aeronautical Information Services (AIS) provide official NOTAM briefings — for example, DFS in Germany, DSNA/SIA in France, ENAIRE in Spain, or NATS in the United Kingdom. The European AIS Database (EAD) operated by Eurocontrol offers a centralised source. Additionally, commercial drone applications such as Droniq, AirMap, DJI FlySafe, and national UTM platforms integrate NOTAM data with interactive maps, making it easier to visualise restrictions relative to a planned flight location. Regardless of the tool used, the remote pilot bears full responsibility for obtaining and understanding all relevant aeronautical information before flying.

Pre-flight planning under EU 2019/947 extends well beyond NOTAM checks. The regulation requires remote pilots to define an operational volume comprising three layers: the flight geography (the intended 3D volume where the drone will fly), a contingency volume (a buffer around the flight geography to contain the drone if something goes wrong), and a ground risk buffer (the area on the ground where the drone could impact if it leaves the contingency volume). Pilots must also calculate battery endurance — considering battery capacity in watt-hours, average power consumption, wind conditions, payload weight, and mandatory reserve margins (typically 20-30% for multirotors). Emergency and contingency procedures must be planned before each flight, including actions for loss of command-and-control link, low battery warnings, fly-away scenarios, and encounters with manned aircraft. When operating near controlled airspace, prior coordination with the relevant Air Traffic Control (ATC) unit or Air Navigation Service Provider (ANSP) is mandatory, and specific authorisations may be required under Article 12 of EU 2019/947.

step_by_step_guide.title: Understand What NOTAMs Are and When They Affect Drone Operations
step_by_step_guide.description: A NOTAM (Notice to Air Missions) is an official notice distributed via aeronautical telecommunication systems to inform pilots of hazards, restrictions, or changes in airspace status. For drone pilots, NOTAMs are critical because they announce temporary flight restrictions (TFRs), military exercises, aerial firefighting zones, VIP movements, and other events that can make flying illegal or dangerous. Under EU 2019/947, remote pilots must check for active NOTAMs before every single flight. A NOTAM can affect your operation even if your planned flight area is normally unrestricted — for example, a temporary restriction for a presidential visit or an air ambulance operation could close the airspace above your location with only hours of notice. Always treat NOTAM checking as a non-negotiable step in your pre-flight routine.

step_by_step_guide.title: Read and Decode a Real NOTAM Using the Standard Format
step_by_step_guide.description: NOTAMs follow the ICAO standard format. Study the sample NOTAM below and learn to identify each field. The Q-line provides a quick summary with coded qualifiers. Field A identifies the affected FIR or aerodrome. Field B gives the start time in UTC (format: YYMMDDHHmm). Field C gives the end time or 'PERM' for permanent. Field D provides a daily schedule if applicable. Field E is the free-text description — the most important field for understanding what is actually restricted. Fields F and G (when present) give lower and upper altitude limits. Practice reading several NOTAMs from your national AIS until you can quickly extract the key information: WHERE is the restriction, WHEN is it active, and WHAT is restricted.
step_by_step_guide.code:
SAMPLE NOTAM:
─────────────────────────────────────────────
A1234/24 NOTAMN
Q) EDGG/QRRCA/IV/BO/W/000/020/5012N00843E005
A) EDGG (Frankfurt FIR)
B) 2024-06-15T08:00 UTC
C) 2024-06-15T17:00 UTC
D) 0800-1700
E) TEMPORARY RESTRICTED AREA ESTABLISHED
   FOR DRONE LIGHT SHOW EVENT.
   RADIUS 5 KM CENTRED ON 5012N 00843E.
   ALL FLIGHTS PROHIBITED EXCEPT
   AUTHORISED PARTICIPANTS.
F) GND (Ground level)
G) 2000 FT AMSL
─────────────────────────────────────────────
Field Key:
  Q-line → FIR / Subject (QRRCA=Restricted Area)
           / Traffic / Purpose / Scope
           / Lower alt / Upper alt / Coordinates+Radius
  A → Affected FIR or aerodrome
  B → Begin date/time (UTC)
  C → Cease date/time (UTC)
  D → Daily schedule
  E → Plain-text description (READ THIS CAREFULLY)
  F → Lower altitude limit
  G → Upper altitude limit

step_by_step_guide.title: Check NOTAMs and Airspace Restrictions for a Planned Flight Location
step_by_step_guide.description: Before each flight, use at least one official source to check for active NOTAMs. Start with your national AIS website — for example, DFS AIS portal (Germany), SIA (France), ENAV (Italy), or ENAIRE (Spain). Enter your planned flight coordinates or aerodrome identifier and request a NOTAM briefing for your planned date and time window. Cross-reference with a drone-specific app like Droniq, AirMap, or your national UTM platform, which overlays NOTAMs on a map so you can visually see if your planned location falls within a restriction. Always check for NOTAMs on the day of the flight, as new ones can be issued with very short notice. Document which NOTAMs you reviewed in your flight log as evidence of compliance.

step_by_step_guide.title: Define Your Operational Volume
step_by_step_guide.description: EU 2019/947 requires you to define an operational volume for every flight. This volume has three concentric layers: the flight geography, the contingency volume, and the ground risk buffer. The flight geography is the 3D space where you intend to fly the drone under normal conditions. The contingency volume surrounds the flight geography and provides space for the drone to be safely recovered if it deviates from its intended path — for example, due to a GPS glitch or sudden wind gust. The ground risk buffer is the area on the ground beyond the contingency volume where third parties could be at risk if the drone crashes. Sketch your operational volume on a map before each flight, ensuring it does not overlap with restricted areas, populated zones, or obstacles.
step_by_step_guide.code:
OPERATIONAL VOLUME (Cross-Section View):

         ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ ← Max altitude
         │     CONTINGENCY VOLUME          │
         │  ┌───────────────────────────┐  │
         │  │                           │  │
         │  │    FLIGHT GEOGRAPHY       │  │
         │  │   (normal flight area)    │  │
         │  │       ✈ Drone path        │  │
         │  │                           │  │
         │  └───────────────────────────┘  │
         │   (buffer for deviations)       │
─────────┴─────────────────────────────────┴─────────
 ◄──GRB──►                                 ◄──GRB──►

 GRB = Ground Risk Buffer (impact area on ground)

 Three Layers:
 1. Flight Geography    → Where drone WILL fly
 2. Contingency Volume  → Where drone MIGHT go
                           if something goes wrong
 3. Ground Risk Buffer  → Ground area at risk
                           if drone falls from
                           contingency volume

step_by_step_guide.title: Calculate Battery Endurance and Plan Flight Time with Safety Margins
step_by_step_guide.description: Battery endurance is one of the most critical elements of flight planning. Start by determining your battery's capacity in watt-hours (Wh) — this is voltage (V) multiplied by amp-hours (Ah). Then estimate your drone's average power consumption in watts (W), which depends on the aircraft weight, payload, wind conditions, and flight profile (hover vs. forward flight). Divide the usable capacity by the consumption rate to get the maximum flight time. Always apply a reserve margin: for multirotors, retain at least 20% reserve for return-to-home and landing. In adverse conditions (strong winds, cold temperatures, heavy payload), increase the reserve to 30% or more. Never plan to use 100% of your battery — lithium polymer batteries can suffer voltage sag and unexpected drops in capacity, especially in cold weather.
step_by_step_guide.code:
BATTERY ENDURANCE CALCULATION:
─────────────────────────────────────────────
 Battery Capacity:
   Wh = Voltage (V) × Amp-hours (Ah)
   Example: 22.2V × 5.0Ah = 111 Wh

 Average Power Consumption:
   Depends on weight, wind, payload, flight mode
   Example: 185 W (hover, light wind)

 Maximum Flight Time:
   T_max = (Wh / W) × 60 minutes
   T_max = (111 / 185) × 60 = 36 minutes

 Usable Flight Time (with 20% reserve):
   T_usable = T_max × 0.80
   T_usable = 36 × 0.80 = 28.8 minutes

 Cold Weather Adjustment (below 5°C):
   T_adjusted = T_usable × 0.85
   T_adjusted = 28.8 × 0.85 = 24.5 minutes
─────────────────────────────────────────────
 RULE: Never plan missions exceeding T_usable.
 Always have enough battery to Return-To-Home.

step_by_step_guide.title: Create a Pre-Flight Checklist Covering Weather, Airspace, NOTAMs, and Equipment
step_by_step_guide.description: A systematic pre-flight checklist ensures nothing is overlooked. Your checklist should cover five areas: (1) Weather — check wind speed, gusts, visibility, precipitation, and temperature against your drone's operating limits; (2) Airspace — verify your flight area is in permitted airspace for your category and that no CTR, restricted, or prohibited areas overlap your operational volume; (3) NOTAMs — review all active NOTAMs for your area and time window; (4) Equipment — inspect the drone, batteries, controller, propellers, and any payload for damage or defects; (5) Regulatory compliance — confirm registration, insurance, operator ID marking, and pilot competency certificate are current. Run through the checklist methodically before every flight, even routine ones.
step_by_step_guide.code:
PRE-FLIGHT CHECKLIST TEMPLATE:
═══════════════════════════════════════════
 DATE: ___________  TIME: ___________
 LOCATION: ____________  GPS: ___________
 PILOT: ____________  REG #: ___________

 [ ] 1. WEATHER
     [ ] Wind speed ≤ drone max (___m/s)
     [ ] Gusts checked (max ___m/s)
     [ ] Visibility > 1 km
     [ ] No precipitation
     [ ] Temperature within limits

 [ ] 2. AIRSPACE
     [ ] Flight area classified: ________
     [ ] Not in CTR/restricted/prohibited
     [ ] Max height confirmed: ___m AGL
     [ ] Authorisation obtained (if needed)

 [ ] 3. NOTAMs
     [ ] NOTAMs checked via: ___________
     [ ] Active NOTAMs noted: __________
     [ ] No conflict with flight plan

 [ ] 4. EQUIPMENT
     [ ] Drone visual inspection: OK
     [ ] Propellers secure and undamaged
     [ ] Battery charged: ___%  (___V)
     [ ] Battery 2 ready: ___%  (___V)
     [ ] Controller charged & bound
     [ ] Firmware up to date
     [ ] SD card / storage ready

 [ ] 5. COMPLIANCE
     [ ] Operator registration valid
     [ ] Drone marked with operator ID
     [ ] Insurance current
     [ ] Competency certificate valid
     [ ] Flight log prepared
═══════════════════════════════════════════
 PILOT SIGNATURE: _______________________

step_by_step_guide.title: Plan Emergency Procedures and Contingency Actions
step_by_step_guide.description: Before every flight, define clear emergency procedures for foreseeable failure scenarios. At a minimum, plan responses for: (1) Loss of command-and-control link — what will the drone do automatically (RTH, hover, land)? Verify the failsafe settings match the environment. (2) Low battery warning — at what percentage will you initiate return? Where is your emergency landing zone? (3) Fly-away — what is your escalation procedure? Can you trigger an emergency motor stop safely? (4) Encounter with manned aircraft — you must always give way and descend or land immediately. (5) Deteriorating weather — define wind and visibility thresholds that will trigger an abort. Write down these procedures and brief any crew members or observers before the flight. If operating under EASA Specific Category (SORA), a formal emergency response plan is mandatory.

quiz.question: What does the abbreviation NOTAM stand for?
options: Notice to Air Missions
Notification of Air Movement
Notice to Aircraft Mechanics
Notification of Airspace Management
quiz.answer: Notice to Air Missions

quiz.question: Which field in a standard NOTAM contains the plain-text description of the restriction or hazard?
options: A-field (Location)
B-field (Begin time)
E-field (Free-text description)
Q-line (Qualifier line)
quiz.answer: E-field (Free-text description)

quiz.question: What is the primary purpose of the Q-line in a NOTAM?
options: To provide the pilot's qualifications required
To give a coded summary of the notice's scope, location, and subject
To list the quality standards for the aerodrome
To indicate the questionnaire for airspace access
quiz.answer: To give a coded summary of the notice's scope, location, and subject

quiz.question: Where can a drone pilot in Europe check for active NOTAMs before a flight?
options: Only through the drone manufacturer's website
National AIS portals, the EAD (Eurocontrol), or drone apps like Droniq/AirMap
Exclusively through Air Traffic Control by phone
Only by visiting the nearest airport in person
quiz.answer: National AIS portals, the EAD (Eurocontrol), or drone apps like Droniq/AirMap

quiz.question: According to EU 2019/947, what are the three components of an operational volume?
options: Takeoff zone, cruise zone, and landing zone
Inner circle, outer circle, and safety perimeter
Flight geography, contingency volume, and ground risk buffer
Climb segment, en-route segment, and descent segment
quiz.answer: Flight geography, contingency volume, and ground risk buffer

quiz.question: What is the minimum recommended battery reserve margin for multirotor drone flight planning?
Under EU 2019/947, when is prior coordination with ATC required for drone operations?
options: Only when flying above 500 metres
When operating near or within controlled airspace
Only for drones weighing more than 25 kg
Coordination is never required in the Open Category
quiz.answer: When operating near or within controlled airspace

quiz.question: Which of the following is NOT a required element of pre-flight planning for drone operations under EASA regulations?
options: Checking active NOTAMs for the flight area
Filing an IFR flight plan with Eurocontrol
Assessing weather conditions against drone operating limits
Defining emergency and contingency procedures
quiz.answer: Filing an IFR flight plan with Eurocontrol

quiz.question: A NOTAM establishes a temporary restricted area from GND to FL050 within a 10 km radius. Your planned drone flight is at 80 metres AGL, 3 km from the centre of the restriction. What should you do?
options: Fly as planned because drones are exempt from NOTAMs
Fly below 50 metres to stay under the restriction
Do not fly — your operation falls within the restricted area's horizontal and vertical boundaries
Contact the drone manufacturer for special clearance
quiz.answer: Do not fly — your operation falls within the restricted area's horizontal and vertical boundaries

quiz.question: What does the 'contingency volume' in the operational volume concept specifically account for?
options: The area where spectators should gather to watch the drone
The space needed for the drone to be recovered if it deviates from the flight geography due to abnormal conditions
The volume of airspace reserved for other aircraft to pass through
The storage space required for spare batteries and equipment
quiz.answer: The space needed for the drone to be recovered if it deviates from the flight geography due to abnormal conditions

learning_script.title: Introduction: Why Flight Planning Separates Safe Pilots from Reckless Ones
learning_script.content:
Every experienced aviator will tell you the same thing: a successful flight is won or lost on the ground, long before the aircraft ever leaves it. This principle applies just as strongly to drone operations as it does to commercial aviation. Flight planning is not bureaucratic paperwork — it is the process that ensures you, the people around you, and other airspace users remain safe. The difference between a professional drone pilot and a reckless one is not the quality of their drone or the steadiness of their hands on the sticks. It is the thoroughness of their preparation.

Under the European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) regulatory framework, specifically EU 2019/947, remote pilots bear personal responsibility for ensuring that every flight is conducted safely and in compliance with applicable rules. This means you must check weather conditions, verify airspace status, review Notices to Air Missions (NOTAMs), assess ground risks, calculate battery endurance, define your operational volume, and plan emergency procedures — all before you power on your drone. Skipping any of these steps is not just careless; it can be illegal.

Consider a real-world scenario: you arrive at a scenic location to capture aerial footage for a client. The sky is clear, the wind is calm, and there are no buildings or people nearby. You think conditions are perfect. But what you did not check was the NOTAM issued that morning establishing a temporary flight restriction for a military exercise directly above your location. If you launch your drone, you are operating illegally and potentially endangering military aircraft. This is not a hypothetical situation — incidents like this happen regularly across Europe.

In this lesson, we will systematically build your flight planning skills, starting with NOTAMs and working through every element of a thorough pre-flight process. By the end, you will have a complete, repeatable workflow that keeps you safe, legal, and professional. Think of flight planning as your most important piece of safety equipment — one that costs nothing but your time and attention.
key_takeaways: Flight planning is the foundation of safe and legal drone operations under EASA regulations
Remote pilots are personally responsible for checking all conditions before every flight
Failing to plan can result in safety incidents, legal consequences, and damage to the drone industry

learning_script.title: NOTAMs Explained: Purpose, Types, and Relevance for UAS Operators
learning_script.content:
A Notice to Air Missions — commonly known by its abbreviation NOTAM — is an official notice issued through aeronautical telecommunication channels to inform pilots of conditions that could affect flight safety. NOTAMs are part of the Aeronautical Information Service (AIS) system established by the International Civil Aviation Organisation (ICAO) and are used worldwide. The term was updated from 'Notice to Airmen' to the gender-neutral 'Notice to Air Missions' by ICAO in 2021, and EASA has adopted this terminology.

NOTAMs serve multiple purposes. They can announce temporary flight restrictions (TFRs) for events like heads-of-state visits, airshows, sporting events, or emergency operations such as firefighting or search and rescue. They can warn about hazards such as crane erections near aerodromes, laser light emissions, bird activity, or volcanic ash. They can notify pilots about changes to aerodrome facilities, navigation aids being out of service, or runway closures. Some NOTAMs establish temporary restricted or danger areas where flight is prohibited or requires special authorisation.

For drone pilots, the most relevant NOTAM categories include: temporary restricted areas (which may cover your planned flight location), warnings about military exercises or live firing ranges, airspace changes due to special events, and notices about other UAS activity in the area. Even if you are flying in uncontrolled airspace well below 120 metres, a NOTAM can still affect you. For example, a temporary restricted area established for a VIP visit typically extends from ground level upwards and would prohibit your drone flight regardless of altitude.

NOTAMs are classified by their urgency and scope. A NOTAMN is a new notice, a NOTAMR replaces a previous one, and a NOTAMC cancels an existing notice. They have defined validity periods — some last only a few hours, while others remain active for months. SNOWTAM and ASHTAM are specialised types for snow/ice conditions and volcanic ash respectively, which are less relevant for typical drone operations but worth knowing about. As a remote pilot, you must develop the habit of checking NOTAMs every time you fly, even at locations you have flown from many times before. Airspace status can change at any time.
key_takeaways: NOTAMs are official notices alerting pilots to hazards, restrictions, or changes affecting airspace
Temporary flight restrictions announced by NOTAM can prohibit drone flights even in normally unrestricted areas
NOTAMN (new), NOTAMR (replacement), and NOTAMC (cancellation) indicate the type of action
Drone pilots must check NOTAMs before every flight, even at familiar locations

learning_script.title: Decoding a NOTAM: The Q-Line, Location, Validity, and Content Fields
learning_script.content:
Reading a NOTAM for the first time can feel like trying to decode a foreign language, but the format is highly structured and becomes straightforward with practice. Every NOTAM follows the ICAO standard format defined in Annex 15 and Document 8126. Understanding this structure allows you to quickly extract the essential information: what is the restriction, where does it apply, and when is it active.

The NOTAM begins with an identifier (e.g., A1234/24) followed by the type indicator (NOTAMN for new, NOTAMR for replacement, NOTAMC for cancellation). The Q-line (qualifier line) is the first structured field and provides a coded summary. It contains the FIR (Flight Information Region) identifier, the NOTAM code indicating the subject and condition, the traffic type (I for IFR, V for VFR, IV for both), the purpose code, the scope, lower and upper altitude limits in flight levels, and the geographical coordinates with a radius in nautical miles. The Q-line is designed for automated filtering — air traffic systems use it to select relevant NOTAMs for a given flight.

Field A identifies the affected location, typically a FIR or aerodrome ICAO code. Field B gives the effective start date and time in UTC, formatted as a 10-digit group (YYMMDDHHmm). Field C provides the end date and time, or the word 'PERM' if the change is permanent, or 'EST' if the end time is estimated. Field D appears when the restriction follows a daily schedule — for example, active only during certain hours. This field helps you determine if your planned flight time falls within the active period.

Field E is the most important field for practical understanding. It contains the free-text description in plain English (or the language of the issuing state) explaining exactly what the notice is about. Read this field carefully — it tells you the nature of the restriction, the specific area affected, any exceptions, and contact information if applicable. Fields F and G, when present, specify the lower and upper vertical limits of the affected airspace. For drone pilots, pay special attention when F states 'GND' or 'SFC' (surface), as this means the restriction starts at ground level and will definitely affect your operation if you are within the horizontal boundaries.
key_takeaways: The Q-line provides a coded summary for automated filtering, including FIR, subject, traffic type, and coordinates
Fields B and C define when the NOTAM is active — always in UTC time
Field E contains the plain-text description and is the most critical field for understanding the restriction
Fields F and G define vertical limits — 'GND' or 'SFC' means the restriction starts at ground level

learning_script.title: Digital Tools: Where and How to Check NOTAMs and Airspace for Drone Flights
learning_script.content:
While understanding the raw NOTAM format is essential knowledge, modern drone pilots benefit from a range of digital tools that make NOTAM checking faster and more intuitive. These tools range from official government AIS portals to commercial drone flight planning applications. A professional approach uses multiple sources to cross-reference and ensure nothing is missed.

Every EU member state operates a national Aeronautical Information Service that publishes NOTAMs. In Germany, the DFS (Deutsche Flugsicherung) AIS portal provides searchable NOTAM briefings. France uses the SIA (Service de l'Information Aéronautique) managed by DSNA. Spain has ENAIRE's INSIGNIA platform. Italy uses ENAV's portal. The UK (post-Brexit, following similar standards) uses the NATS AIS website. These official sources are the authoritative reference — if there is ever a discrepancy between an app and an official AIS source, the official source takes precedence.

At the European level, Eurocontrol operates the European AIS Database (EAD), which aggregates NOTAM data from across the continent. The EAD allows you to search for NOTAMs by FIR, geographic coordinates, or route. For drone-specific needs, the newer Eurocontrol Network Manager SDO (Static Data Operations) platform is being developed to support U-space services. These centralised European sources are particularly useful when planning flights near national borders, where NOTAMs from adjacent FIRs might be relevant.

Commercial drone apps have transformed NOTAM accessibility. Applications like Droniq (widely used in Germany and expanding across Europe), AirMap, DJI Fly, and various national UTM (Unmanned Traffic Management) platforms overlay NOTAM-derived restrictions on interactive maps. You can enter your planned flight location and instantly see active restrictions, controlled airspace boundaries, and geographical zones. Some apps even provide automated alerts when new NOTAMs are issued for areas you have saved. However, remember that these apps are tools to assist your planning — they do not replace your legal obligation to verify information through authoritative sources.

When checking NOTAMs, follow a systematic process. First, identify your flight location coordinates and the relevant FIR. Second, search for all active NOTAMs in that FIR for your planned date and time. Third, filter for NOTAMs that could affect operations at your altitude (typically below 120 metres AGL for Open Category). Fourth, read the E-field of each relevant NOTAM carefully. Finally, document which NOTAMs you reviewed in your flight log. This documentation demonstrates due diligence and can protect you legally if questions arise later.
key_takeaways: National AIS portals are the authoritative source for NOTAMs in each EU member state
The Eurocontrol EAD provides a centralised European NOTAM database useful for cross-border operations
Drone apps like Droniq and AirMap visualise NOTAMs on maps but do not replace official sources
Always document which NOTAMs you reviewed in your flight log as evidence of compliance

learning_script.title: The Operational Volume Concept: Flight Geography and Contingency Planning
learning_script.content:
The operational volume is a fundamental concept in EASA drone regulations, introduced to provide a structured framework for assessing and mitigating the risks of UAS operations. Defined in EU 2019/947 and its associated Acceptable Means of Compliance (AMC) and Guidance Material (GM), the operational volume consists of three distinct but interconnected components: the flight geography, the contingency volume, and the ground risk buffer. Understanding and correctly defining these components is essential for legal compliance and operational safety.

The flight geography is the three-dimensional volume of airspace within which you intend to operate your drone under normal conditions. It encompasses the entire planned flight path, including takeoff, any waypoints, the maximum planned altitude, and the landing location. When defining your flight geography, consider the accuracy of your navigation system (GPS), the drone's manoeuvrability, and typical environmental factors. The flight geography should be as small as practical — this minimises the area where risks need to be assessed and makes your operation more predictable to other airspace users.

The contingency volume is a buffer zone that surrounds the flight geography. Its purpose is to provide sufficient space for the drone to be brought under control and recovered safely if it deviates from the intended flight path due to abnormal but foreseeable circumstances. Such circumstances include GPS drift, temporary loss of command link (before failsafe activates), unexpected wind gusts, or minor technical malfunctions. The size of the contingency volume depends on the drone's performance characteristics, the response time of the pilot, and the reliability of the containment system (if any). For most Open Category operations with standard consumer drones, a contingency volume of 10 to 30 metres around and above the flight geography is typically adequate, though this should be increased in challenging conditions.

The ground risk buffer extends outward from the contingency volume's horizontal boundaries on the ground. It represents the area where the drone could impact the surface if it were to fall uncontrolled from the edge of the contingency volume. The size of the ground risk buffer depends on the drone's maximum altitude, its ballistic characteristics (weight, drag), and any energy-absorbing systems such as parachutes. Under SORA methodology (used for Specific Category), calculating the ground risk buffer precisely is mandatory. For Open Category operations, while a formal SORA analysis is not required, you should still mentally assess the ground risk buffer to ensure uninvolved persons and property are not at risk. The fundamental principle is simple: your entire operational volume — including all three layers — must be free of conflicts with restricted airspace, and the ground risk buffer must not encompass areas where you cannot guarantee the safety of uninvolved people.
key_takeaways: The flight geography defines where the drone will fly under normal conditions — keep it as small as practical
The contingency volume provides buffer space for recovering the drone during abnormal but foreseeable situations
The ground risk buffer covers the ground area at risk if the drone falls uncontrolled from the contingency volume
All three layers must be free of airspace conflicts and ground risks to uninvolved persons

learning_script.title: Battery Management: Endurance Calculation and Reserve Planning
learning_script.content:
Battery endurance planning is arguably the most critical technical aspect of drone flight planning. Unlike manned aircraft where fuel gauges and range calculations are well-established practices, many drone pilots fly with only a vague sense of how much flight time they have available. This casual approach leads to emergency landings, fly-aways when batteries are depleted mid-flight, and crashes when voltage drops below the level needed to maintain controlled flight. Professional flight planning demands a systematic approach to battery management.

The fundamental calculation starts with battery capacity, expressed in watt-hours (Wh). To calculate watt-hours, multiply the battery's nominal voltage (V) by its capacity in amp-hours (Ah). A typical prosumer drone battery might be rated at 15.4V and 5.87Ah, giving 90.4 Wh. The next variable is your drone's average power consumption in watts, which varies significantly based on total takeoff weight (including payload), flight profile (hovering consumes more power than steady forward flight for multirotors), wind conditions, and ambient temperature. Manufacturers typically publish hover time and forward flight time figures, from which you can estimate average power draw.

Divide the battery capacity by the average power consumption and multiply by 60 to get maximum flight time in minutes. For example, 90.4 Wh divided by 180 W equals 0.502 hours, or approximately 30 minutes. However, you should never plan to use 100 percent of this capacity. Industry best practice and regulatory guidance recommend maintaining a minimum reserve of 20 percent for return-to-home and landing under normal conditions. This means your usable flight time is 30 multiplied by 0.80, equalling 24 minutes.

Environmental factors can dramatically reduce actual endurance. Cold temperatures reduce lithium polymer battery capacity — at 0 degrees Celsius, you may lose 15 to 20 percent of rated capacity. Strong headwinds increase power consumption substantially. High altitude reduces air density, requiring the motors to work harder. A heavy payload increases the power needed to maintain altitude. When planning in adverse conditions, apply an additional 15 to 20 percent reduction on top of your standard reserve. For critical operations, consider performing a timed test flight in similar conditions to measure actual endurance empirically rather than relying solely on calculations.

Always monitor battery voltage during flight, not just the percentage displayed on your controller. Voltage is a more reliable indicator of remaining capacity, especially as the battery ages. Set a voltage-based return-to-home threshold in your drone's settings. For a 4S (4-cell) LiPo battery with a nominal voltage of 14.8V, consider initiating return when voltage drops to around 14.0V, and ensure landing by 13.6V. Flying below the manufacturer's minimum voltage risks permanent battery damage and sudden power loss.
key_takeaways: Calculate battery endurance using the formula: (Wh / W) × 60 = maximum flight time in minutes
Always maintain at least a 20% battery reserve for return-to-home and safe landing
Cold weather, wind, altitude, and payload all reduce actual flight endurance below calculated values
Monitor voltage during flight — it is more reliable than percentage for assessing remaining capacity

learning_script.title: The Pre-Flight Planning Process: A Systematic Approach
learning_script.content:
Pre-flight planning should follow a structured, repeatable process that covers every relevant factor. Professional drone operators do not rely on memory or intuition — they use checklists and systematic procedures to ensure nothing is overlooked. This approach, borrowed from manned aviation, dramatically reduces the risk of incidents caused by simple oversights. Under EASA regulations, the remote pilot is responsible for ensuring that the flight can be conducted safely, which implicitly requires thorough pre-flight planning.

The planning process begins days or even weeks before the flight for complex operations, and at minimum several hours before for routine flights. Start with the mission objective: what are you trying to achieve, and what flight profile does this require? Mapping a construction site requires a different flight plan than filming a real estate property or inspecting a power line. Define the flight area, maximum altitude needed, estimated flight time, and number of battery changes required.

Next, assess the airspace. Identify the classification of the airspace above your planned location. In the EU, most drone operations in the Open Category take place in uncontrolled airspace (Class G) below 120 metres AGL. However, many operational areas are near controlled airspace (CTRs around airports), temporary restricted zones, or national no-fly zones. Check for geographical zones established by your member state under Article 15 of EU 2019/947 — these are permanent or semi-permanent restrictions specific to each country. Then check for active NOTAMs as we discussed in previous pages.

Weather assessment is the next critical step. Check aviation weather forecasts (METARs and TAFs for nearby aerodromes give a good indication) and general weather forecasts for your specific location. Key parameters include wind speed and direction (both surface and at your planned altitude), visibility, cloud base, precipitation, and temperature. Compare these against your drone's published operating limits. Most consumer drones are rated for winds up to 10 to 12 metres per second and temperatures from 0 to 40 degrees Celsius. Do not fly in conditions that approach these limits unless you have significant experience and a compelling reason.

Finally, conduct an equipment check. Inspect the drone airframe for damage, ensure propellers are secure and undamaged, verify that batteries are fully charged and within their service life, test the remote controller, confirm GPS lock and compass calibration, and check any payload equipment. Ensure your remote pilot certificate, operator registration, and insurance documentation are current and accessible. Plan your takeoff and landing locations, identifying flat, clear areas away from obstacles and uninvolved people. With all these elements addressed, you are ready to proceed to your on-site preparations and the flight itself.
key_takeaways: Use a structured, checklist-based process for pre-flight planning — never rely on memory alone
Assess airspace classification, geographical zones, and active NOTAMs before every flight
Check aviation weather forecasts and compare conditions against your drone's operating limits
Verify all equipment, documentation, and regulatory compliance before proceeding to the flight site

learning_script.title: Emergency and Contingency Procedures: Planning for the Unexpected
learning_script.content:
The mark of a truly competent pilot is not how they fly when everything goes right — it is how they respond when things go wrong. Emergency and contingency planning is a mandatory part of responsible drone operation under EASA regulations. For Open Category operations, the pilot must have adequate knowledge of emergency procedures. For Specific Category operations under a SORA assessment, formal emergency response plans are explicitly required. Regardless of category, every flight should have pre-defined responses for foreseeable failure modes.

The most common emergency scenarios for drone operations include loss of command-and-control (C2) link, low battery or unexpected power loss, GPS failure or degraded navigation, motor or propeller failure, fly-away situations, encounter with manned aircraft, and sudden weather deterioration. For each of these scenarios, you should have a planned response that you have thought through before the flight — not one you improvise under stress.

Loss of C2 link is perhaps the most critical scenario for remote pilots. Before every flight, verify your drone's failsafe settings and ensure they are appropriate for the operational environment. Common failsafe options include return-to-home (RTH), hover in place, or land immediately. In an open area away from people, RTH is usually the best option. However, if you are flying in a confined space or near obstacles, an immediate landing might be safer. If your drone is equipped with obstacle avoidance, consider how the failsafe behaviour interacts with these systems. Always set a realistic RTH altitude that clears all obstacles in the flight area.

Fly-away scenarios, where the drone does not respond to any commands and moves away uncontrollably, are rare but serious. If a fly-away occurs, attempt to regain control by cycling through different flight modes and toggling the C2 link. If control cannot be regained, track the drone visually as long as possible and note its direction of travel. If equipped with a flight tracker, monitor its position remotely. Report the incident to the relevant aviation authority promptly. Emergency motor stop (cutting power to all motors) should be considered as a last resort only when the drone is over an unpopulated area, as it will result in an uncontrolled descent.

Encountering manned aircraft is another situation that requires immediate action. Under all circumstances, the drone must give way to manned aircraft. If you see or hear a manned aircraft approaching your area, immediately reduce altitude and if possible move away from the aircraft's flight path. Land the drone if the aircraft is operating at low level near your position. After landing, do not resume flying until you are confident the manned aircraft has departed the area. Document the encounter in your flight log, including the time, type of aircraft if identifiable, and the actions you took. Repeated encounters at a location may indicate that the area is unsuitable for drone operations.
key_takeaways: Pre-define emergency responses for C2 link loss, low battery, fly-away, manned aircraft encounters, and weather changes
Verify and configure failsafe settings (RTH altitude, behaviour) appropriately before every flight
Always give way to manned aircraft — reduce altitude and land if necessary
Document any emergency situations or manned aircraft encounters in your flight log

learning_script.title: Coordination with Other Airspace Users and ATC
learning_script.content:
Drones operate in a shared airspace system that includes commercial airlines, general aviation, military aircraft, helicopters, gliders, paragliders, and other drones. Understanding how to coordinate with other airspace users — and when formal coordination with Air Traffic Control (ATC) is required — is essential for safe and legal operations. The fundamental principle in aviation is 'see and avoid,' and for drone pilots, this translates to maintaining visual line of sight (VLOS) and always being prepared to yield to manned aircraft.

Under EU 2019/947, drone operations in the Open Category are permitted without ATC coordination only when flying in airspace that does not require it — typically uncontrolled airspace (Class G) below the maximum permitted altitude. However, if you need to operate near or within controlled airspace, such as a Control Zone (CTR) around an airport, prior coordination with the relevant Air Navigation Service Provider (ANSP) is mandatory. This coordination typically requires submitting a request to the local ATC unit, specifying your planned operation details: location (GPS coordinates), date, time window, maximum altitude, drone type, and purpose of flight. Many member states have established digital platforms or specific procedures for this coordination.

In some EU member states, geographical zones defined under Article 15 of EU 2019/947 establish specific requirements for operating near aerodromes, heliports, or other sensitive areas. These zones may require you to use electronic identification and geo-awareness systems, obtain specific authorisations, or comply with additional operational conditions. For example, Germany requires a clearance from DFS for drone flights within 1.5 km of an aerodrome, while other countries have different distance thresholds and procedures. Always research the specific requirements of the member state where you plan to operate.

Beyond formal ATC coordination, situational awareness of other airspace users is your responsibility during the entire flight. Keep scanning the sky and listening for approaching aircraft. If you are operating in an area frequented by manned aircraft — such as near a hospital with helicopter operations, in mountain areas popular with glider pilots, or near military training routes — take extra precautions. Consider using an ADS-B receiver to monitor manned aircraft positions in real time, although be aware that not all aircraft transmit ADS-B signals (particularly gliders, ultralights, and some military aircraft).

Future developments in U-space — the European framework for UAS traffic management — will introduce more structured coordination mechanisms. U-space services will include automated flight authorisation, traffic information, and conflict detection for drone operations. Several EU member states are already piloting U-space implementations. As the U-space ecosystem matures, drone pilots will have access to real-time airspace information and dynamic flight approval systems. Until then, manual coordination and thorough pre-flight planning remain your primary tools for safe integration into the shared airspace.
key_takeaways: Formal ATC coordination is required when operating near or within controlled airspace such as CTRs
Each EU member state has specific geographical zones and procedures for operating near aerodromes
Maintain continuous situational awareness for manned aircraft during every flight and always yield right of way
U-space services will eventually automate drone-manned aircraft coordination but manual planning remains essential now

learning_script.title: Summary: Your Complete Pre-Flight Planning Workflow for EASA Compliance
learning_script.content:
Throughout this lesson, you have learned each element of a comprehensive pre-flight planning process. Now let us bring everything together into a single, actionable workflow that you can follow before every drone flight. This workflow ensures compliance with EU 2019/947, protects the safety of all airspace users and people on the ground, and establishes your professionalism as a remote pilot.

Phase 1: Mission Planning (hours to days before flight). Define the mission objective and required flight profile. Identify the flight location and obtain GPS coordinates. Research the airspace classification and any permanent geographical zones or restrictions. Check whether ATC coordination or special authorisations are required. Assess the site for ground risks including proximity to people, buildings, roads, and critical infrastructure. This phase sets the foundation for the entire operation.

Phase 2: Pre-Flight Briefing (1 to 2 hours before flight). Check current and forecast weather conditions — wind, visibility, precipitation, temperature — and compare against drone operating limits. Review all active NOTAMs for the flight area and time window using official AIS sources and cross-referencing with a drone planning app. Define your operational volume: flight geography, contingency volume, and ground risk buffer. Ensure the operational volume does not conflict with any restrictions, obstacles, or populated areas. Calculate battery endurance for the planned flight profile and conditions, applying appropriate reserve margins.

Phase 3: On-Site Preparation (at the flight location, before takeoff). Conduct a physical inspection of the drone, batteries, controller, and any payload. Verify GPS lock and compass calibration. Confirm failsafe settings are correctly configured for the environment (RTH altitude, C2 link loss behaviour). Brief any crew members or observers on the flight plan, emergency procedures, and their roles. Identify the takeoff and landing zone and any emergency landing options. Perform a final weather and NOTAM check. Verify that your registration, operator ID marking, insurance, and competency certificate are all current.

Phase 4: Go/No-Go Decision. Review all gathered information and make a deliberate go or no-go decision. If any element raises concern — weather at limits, unresolved NOTAM, equipment issue, or gut feeling that something is not right — choose no-go. There is no flight so important that it is worth compromising safety. A postponed or cancelled flight is always better than an incident. If everything checks out, proceed with confidence to your flight.

Remember: this process becomes faster and more natural with practice, but it should never be shortened or skipped. Pilots who cut corners in planning are the ones who end up in accident reports. Your systematic approach to flight planning is what makes you a competent, professional remote pilot — and it is exactly what EASA regulators expect from every person who operates a drone in European airspace.
key_takeaways: Follow a four-phase workflow: Mission Planning, Pre-Flight Briefing, On-Site Preparation, and Go/No-Go Decision
Always make a deliberate go or no-go decision based on all available information — when in doubt, do not fly
Document your planning process and NOTAM checks in your flight log for regulatory compliance
Systematic planning becomes faster with practice but must never be shortened or skipped
